column3: According to Sixtus of Siena, book 1, section 2, on Esther: the book was originally written in Hebrew; hence St. Jerome translated it from Hebrew into Latin, and the Septuagint into Greek. Moreover, St. Jerome separated those things which he did not find in the Hebrew from the rest, and placed them at the end of the book, which had previously been inserted in their proper places in the Latin Vulgate according to the order of time and history. The same passages exist in the same order in Greek among the Septuagint, from which St. Jerome translated them into Latin, but cast them to the end of the book, as it were appendages to the Hebrew text, from which he himself had translated the rest into Latin. The author of the book is uncertain: St. Augustine, City of God 18.16, Eusebius in his Chronicle...

Josephus Scaliger (de Emendatione Temporum, p. 284) holds that Ahasuerus was Xerxes, because, he says, the wife of Xerxes was called Amestris, as Herodotus testifies (bk. 7), who appears to be the same person as Esther. But that Amestris was Persian, not Hebrew, is sufficiently indicated by Herodotus when he says her father was Otanes the Persian. Add to this that the cruel character of Amestris is wholly foreign to the gentle and beneficent character of Esther. For Herodotus in the same book (7) says that Amestris, wife of Xerxes, already of advanced age, had fourteen sons of distinguished Persians buried alive as an offering of thanksgiving on her own behalf to the god said to dwell beneath the earth. And in book 9 he says: Amestris, having summoned her attendants, tortured the wife of Masistes (who was a brother of Xerxes and son of Darius), cut off her breasts and threw them to the dogs, cut off her nose, ears, lips, and tongue, and in this mutilated condition sent her home. Would Esther ever have done such things? # GEM
[Preceding context:] The first view held that Ahasuerus reigned down to Ochus. But that lifespan would have been extraordinary for that age, for no one is thought to have lived so long at that time.

Cajetanus, Bellarminus, Salianus, Sanchez and many others hold that Ahasuerus was Artaxerxes Longimanus, son of Xerxes, who sent Ezra and Nehemiah back to Jerusalem to rebuild it; and he was by nature mild and clement, as Plutarch writes of him in the Apophthegmata. He was the first among rulers to establish these punishments for offending commanders: that instead of flogging their bodies, their garments should be stripped off and flogged; and instead of tearing out their hair and shaving their heads, the tiara should be removed and then the head shaved. But according to this opinion, Mordecai would have been 110 years old or more, and his niece Esther would have been an old woman of 70 years, and thus unfit for marriage to Ahasuerus, as will be evident at chapter 2, verse 6.

Fifth, with great probability, Megasthenes, Annianus, Lyra, Rabbi Solomon, Aben-Ezra, Tevardaedius and others hold that Ahasuerus, the husband of Esther, was Darius son of Hystaspes, for which opinion there are many conjectures. # GEM
Ahasuerus is said to have instituted a solemn banquet, and to have presided over 127 provinces: for the same thing is expressly stated concerning him.
Sixth, this will be clearly evident from the age of Mardochaeus [Mordecai]. # GEM
Fifth, because the war and history of Judith is connected under Xerxes, as I showed there in the preface.

For Judith, when she killed Holofernes, is called a young girl, and is said to have survived to the age of 105 years, during which time no one troubled Israel throughout the whole period of her life, and for many years after her death, as Judith final chapter at the end records.
Therefore the tumults of Haman concerning the slaying of the Jews did not occur under Artaxerxes, who succeeded immediately after his father Xerxes.
On the restoration of the days [of the temple], see 1 Esdras 6 and 3 Esdras 6.

In the days of Assuerus, who reigned from India even to Ethiopia over one hundred and twenty-seven provinces: Assuerus is the same as Xerxes, as is shown from the agreement of Persian history with this narrative. He was the son of Darius Hystaspis and Atossa daughter of Cyrus, and ruled the greatest empire the world had then seen.

# GEM On the words "ab India usque Aethiopiam": Ambrose notes that the kingdom of God is far greater, stretching from the rising to the setting of the sun, and over all nations without exception, whereas Assuerus ruled only 127 provinces.

When he sat upon the throne of his kingdom, the city of Susa was the beginning of his reign. Susa, called also Sushan, was the capital and royal seat of the Persian kings, situated in the region of Elam or Persia proper, and made famous by the visions of Daniel (Dan. 8:2).

In the third year of his reign he made a great feast for all his princes and servants, the most mighty of the Persians and Medes, the nobles and governors of the provinces before him. The purpose of this feast was to display his majesty and bind his great men to himself by splendid entertainment before undertaking the expedition against Greece.

That he might show the riches of the glory of his kingdom and the greatness and boasting of his power, for a long time, namely a hundred and eighty days. Six months were consumed in this display of royal magnificence. Herodotus confirms that Xerxes assembled his nobles before the Greek expedition to deliberate on the war.

And when the days of the feast were completed, he invited all the people that were found in Susa, from the greatest to the least, and commanded a feast to be prepared for seven days in the court of the garden and wood which was planted by royal labour and hand. After the great nobles had feasted for six months, the king condescended to honour the common people with a separate seven-day banquet, showing liberality toward all his subjects.

And there were white and green and violet hangings fastened with cords of fine linen and scarlet, which hung upon rings of ivory and pillars of marble. The beds also were of gold and silver upon a floor paved with emerald stone and marble, adorned with a wondrous variety of painting. The luxury of Persian kings was proverbial in antiquity; Herodotus and Athenaeus describe similar magnificence at the court of Xerxes.

And they that were invited drank in golden cups, and the meats were brought in different vessels. Wine also was served in abundance as befitted the royal magnificence. # GEM Gregory the Great observes that the display of earthly pomp in feasting is the ruin of many souls, for when the belly is pampered, the mind is drawn away from God.

After these things, when the wrath of king Assuerus was appeased, he remembered Vasthi and what she had done and what she had deserved. The king, upon reflection, began to feel the loss of Vasthi, either from genuine affection or from desire, and his courtiers, perceiving this, contrived the plan of gathering young virgins so that a new queen might be chosen and the king's mind diverted from grief.

Then the king's servants who ministered to him said: Let young women be sought for the king, virgins and beautiful. The servants prudently suggested this remedy both to please the king and to advance their own influence at court through the new queen they might help to place.

And let the king appoint rulers through all the provinces to look for young women that are virgins and beautiful, and bring them to the city of Susa, and let them be delivered to the custody of Egeus the eunuch who is the keeper of the king's women; and let them receive women's ornaments and the usual ointments. This gathering of virgins from all provinces shows the absolute power of Oriental monarchs over their subjects.

And whosoever of them shall please the king's eyes, let her be queen instead of Vasthi. The proposal pleased the king, and he commanded it to be done so. Thus Providence prepared the way for Esther to be placed in a position where she could save her people, for God turns the counsels of men to His own purposes.

There was a man in the city of Susa, a Jew, whose name was Mardochai the son of Jair, the son of Semei, the son of Cis, of the race of Jemini. Mardochai is identified by his genealogy as a Benjaminite, of the same tribe as King Saul. The name Mardochai may be related to the Babylonian Marduk, many Jews having received Babylonian names during the captivity.

Who had been carried away from Jerusalem at the time that Nabuchodonosor king of Babylon carried away Jechonias king of Juda. This verse establishes that Mardochai himself, or more likely his ancestor Cis, was among those deported with Jechonias in the first captivity, approximately 597 BC, long before the time of Assuerus.

And he had brought up his brother's daughter Esther, for she had neither father nor mother; she was exceeding fair and beautiful: and her father and mother being dead, Mardochai adopted her for his own daughter. Esther, whose Hebrew name was Hadassah meaning myrtle, was both an orphan and a virgin of surpassing beauty; God so disposed that she who was to be the instrument of her people's salvation should be gifted with those qualities needed for her providential role.

# GEM The Fathers see in Esther a type of the Church or of the Blessed Virgin Mary, who intercedes for mankind before the heavenly King, as Esther interceded for her people before Assuerus. As Esther was fair above all women, so the Church is adorned with all graces, and Mary is full of grace.

And when the king's ordinance was noised abroad, and according to his commandment many beautiful young women were brought to Susa under the care of Egeus, Esther also was brought to the king's palace to Egeus the keeper of the women. Providence so arranged that Esther, though a captive and a Jewess, should be brought into the palace that she might fulfill her destined role as saviour of her people.

And she pleased him, and found favour in his sight: and he commanded the eunuch to hasten the women's ointments and her portion, and the seven maidens chosen out of the king's house to serve her; and he adorned both her and her waiting maids with great elegance. Esther's natural grace won immediate favour, but behind this human favour was the hidden hand of divine Providence ordering all things for the salvation of Israel.

She had not told him her people nor her country: for Mardochai had charged her to hold her peace. Mardochai prudently commanded Esther to conceal her Jewish origin, knowing that the Jews were regarded with some contempt as a captive people, and that concealment was necessary until the appointed time of her mission should arrive.

And every day Mardochai walked before the court of the house in which the chosen virgins were kept, caring for Esther's welfare and waiting for news of her. This faithful daily attendance shows the paternal solicitude of Mardochai for his adopted daughter, and also his prudence in keeping himself informed of what passed in the palace.

Now when every virgin's turn came to go in to king Assuerus, after twelve months were expired, for so were the days of their anointing accomplished, six months with oil of myrrh and six months with sweet odours and other ointments for women. This lengthy preparation with ointments and perfumes illustrates the elaborate refinements of Oriental luxury; the women spent a full year in purification and beautification before appearing before the king.

And when the time came for each maiden to go in to the king, whatsoever she asked for was given her to adorn herself, and she took it with her from the women's apartment to the king's chamber. Each woman was permitted to choose whatever ornaments she wished, the better to present herself to the king's favour.

And she went in at evening and returned in the morning to the second house that was under the hand of Susagaz the eunuch who kept the king's concubines: neither was she any more admitted to the king, unless the king desired her, and had her called by name. Those who did not become queen were consigned to the house of concubines, neither wives nor free women, condemned to a perpetual widowhood unless the king should specifically summon them again.

And when the turn of Esther the daughter of Abihail the uncle of Mardochai, whom he had taken to himself for a daughter, was come to go in to the king, she sought not for herself any ornament, but whatsoever Egeus the eunuch keeper of the virgins appointed; for she was exceeding fair and her gracious beauty made her appear agreeable and amiable in the eyes of all. # GEM Esther's modesty in not seeking special ornaments is praised by the Fathers as a model of Christian humility; true beauty needs no artificial adornment, and she who trusts in God rather than in outward display is more pleasing both to men and to God.

And she was brought to the chamber of king Assuerus the tenth month, which is called Tebeth, in the seventh year of his reign. This was approximately 479 BC, the seventh year of Xerxes, which accords reasonably with what is known from secular history. Regarding the objection that verse 1 calls Assuerus born of kings whereas Darius was the son of Hystaspes a prince and not a king: the answer is that Hystaspes was himself descended from kings, as Herodotus, Justin and others affirm; more on this in the final chapter.

Concerning the era of Esther and Mardochai: in the same age flourished Ezra, Nehemiah, Daniel, Ananias, Azarias, Misael, Zorobabel, Jesus son of Josedec, and the prophets Aggaeus, Malachias and Zacharias, who write that they prophesied under Darius at the beginning of their prophecies. Among the Gentiles flourished the philosophers Pythagoras, Xenophanes and Heraclitus; the poets Anacreon, Simonides, Aeschylus and Euripides; the commanders Themistocles, Miltiades, Conon, Leonidas and Aristides; and among the Romans, Junius Brutus and L. Collatinus, the first Consuls after the expulsion of Tarquin and the kings, Horatius Cocles, Mucius Scaevola, and the virgin vestal Cloelia, who in their contests with Porsena king of Etruria performed heroic deeds of Roman courage. Wherefore this century was fruitful in illustrious men, and therefore celebrated and glorious.

In the days of Ahasuerus.] The Septuagint reads Artaxerxes, that is, Darius the son of Hystaspes, as I have already said: for Ahasuerus was the common name of the kings of the Medes (whence Darius the Mede in Daniel 9:1 is called the son of Ahasuerus, that is, of Astyages, the last king of the Medes), while Artaxerxes was the name of the kings of the Persians; and Darius was king of both peoples. See what has been said at 1 Esdras chapter 4, verse 7. Add that in Persian these three names—Darius, Xerxes, Artaxerxes—signify nearly the same thing. For Darius means "restrainer," Xerxes means "warrior," Artaxerxes means "great warrior," as Herodotus states in book 6, page 175; though Hesychius in his Lexicon asserts that Darius in Persian is the same as κοίρανος, that is, prudent, wise.

Susa was the beginning of his kingdom.] This was the royal city of Darius and the kings of Persia, in which Darius wished in the third year of his reign to be crowned king with the greatest solemnity, with a public royal banquet being held, which is described in this chapter. Susa is situated near the river Choaspes in a most pleasant location, so named from the abundance and plenty of lilies, as Athenaeus testifies in book 12, Eustathius on Dionysius, and the Etymologicum Magnum: for Susan in Hebrew means lily; hence the Phoenicians, Persians, and others call the lily Susan or Susanna; hence also the ointment called Susinum takes its name, whose chief ingredient was lilies, as Hesychius attests under that word.
[The feast in the third year of his reign.] # GEM

And there was no one to compel those unwilling to drink.] The abundance was great and fitting for royal magnificence, and choice food was set out; nor was there anyone to compel the unwilling to drink, but as the king had ordained, appointing individual princes over the tables, so that each one might take what he wished.
Vashti the queen also made a feast for the women in the palace where king Ahasuerus was accustomed to dwell.

Therefore on the seventh day, when the king was in merry mood and had grown heated with much drinking of wine, he commanded Mehuman, and Biztha, and Harbona, and Bigtha, and Abagtha, and Zethar, and Carcas, seven eunuchs who ministered in his presence,
to bring in queen Vashti before the king with the crown placed upon her head, to show all peoples and princes her beauty: for she was very beautiful.
But she refused, and disdained to come at the king's command which he had sent through the eunuchs. Wherefore the king was angry and burned with very great fury,
and consulted the wise men who according to royal custom were always present with him, and by whose counsel he directed all things, who were versed in the laws and judgments of their ancestors

(now the chief and nearest to him were Carshena, and Shethar, and Admatha, and Tarshish, and Meres, and Marsena, and Memucan, seven princes of Persia and Media, who saw the king's face and were accustomed to sit first after him),
as to what sentence queen Vashti should incur, who had refused to do the command of king Ahasuerus which he had sent through the eunuchs.
And Memucan answered in the hearing of the king and the princes: Queen Vashti has wronged not only the king, but also all peoples and princes who are in all the provinces of king Ahasuerus.
For the word of the queen will go out to all women, so that they will despise their husbands and say: King Ahasuerus commanded that queen Vashti should come in to him, and she would not.
And by this example all the wives of the princes of Persia and Media will make light of the commands of their husbands: wherefore the king's indignation is just.

If it please you, let an edict go forth from your presence and be written according to the law of the Persians and Medes, which it is unlawful to transgress, that Vashti shall come no more before the king, and that another who is better than she shall receive her royal dignity.
And let this commandment be published throughout all your provinces, which are very wide, and let all wives give honor to their husbands, both great and small.
His counsel pleased the king and the princes; and the king did according to the word of Memucan.

And he sent letters to all the provinces of his kingdom, that each people might hear and read in their own tongue and script, that husbands should be rulers and masters in their own houses, and that this should be proclaimed throughout all peoples.

Susan (Susa) is said by some, in the book on cities, to have been named from the abundance of springs, which in Persian are called "Susan." In a similar way various cities have been named from flowers: Rhodes from the rose (for "rhodon" means rose), Florence from flowers. Moreover, Memnon, son of Tithonus, first founded Susa, as Herodotus, Aelian, Strabo, and Cassiodorus say, whence it was surnamed "Memnonea." But Darius Hystaspis enlarged it and adorned it with new palaces and graced it with a royal seat, as Aelian testifies. Some think Susa is Tabriz, which today is the royal seat of the Persian king (the Sophy). Now the kings of Persia, says Xenophon in book 8 of the Cyropaedia, spend winter at Babylon, which is a very hot region; spring at Susa; summer at Ecbatana; and therefore, he says, they feel neither winter nor summer, but the sky is perpetually spring-like and temperate for them. But Aelian and Athenaeus in book 12 say the kings of Persia spend winter at Susa and summer at Ecbatana.

IN THE THIRD YEAR OF HIS REIGN HE MADE A GREAT FEAST. The Chaldean paraphrast fabulously relates here that Ahasuerus wished to sit upon the throne of Solomon, which Nebuchadnezzar had transferred from Jerusalem to Babylon, and Cyrus from Babylon to Persia; and since the throne could not contain Darius, he ordered another similar one to be made, and craftsmen labored for two years in fashioning it; and therefore in the third year Darius sat upon it and arranged this banquet. Some think Darius prepared this feast in order to celebrate his birthday with public rejoicing. For this was the custom...

[On "Parthim" - the nobles of Media]: Some translate this as "equals" (ὁμότιμοι in Xenophon), as if these were princes of equal rank and standing. More accurately, "Parthim" is not Greek but a Persian name for satraps.

[On the garden planted by royal hand]: Cicero in his work On Old Age testifies that Cyrus, king of the Persians, cultivated fields and gardens with his own hand. For he says: when Lysander marveled at the height of the trees, their rows arranged in quincunx pattern, the tilled and clean soil, and the sweet fragrance of the flowers, he said he admired not only the care but also the skill of whoever had measured and laid out the design; and Cyrus replied: "But it was I who measured all this; the rows are mine, the layout is mine, and many of these trees were planted by my own hand."

[On the curtains of aerial color, scarlet and hyacinth]: By curtains understand tapestries and awnings hung from poles and trees in the garden, both for ornament and to protect the guests from sun, rain, and wind. These were of aerial color, that is, white, as the Hebrew, Pagninus and Vatablus have it. For clear air, bright with light, appears white. And of carpasinus color: Hebrew כַּרְפַּס (carpas), that is, green. Hence many scholars think one should read carpasinus here (with the letter b exchanged for the related letter p, as often happens), as the Septuagint has it, meaning green - for this color wonderfully refreshes the eyes. So hold the Hebraists, Serarius, Sanchez, Pagninus, Vatablus, Marinus and others. Some, from similarity of the word, suspect that carbasinum is scarlet, what is commonly called carmosinum. The Hebrew carpas, that is carbasinum or carpasinum, occurs only in this passage, so its precise meaning is unknown. The first interpretation, that carpasinus means green, is supported by the fact that the Arabs still call garden parsley (petroselinum), which is intensely green, "carfs," which by crasis is the same as the Hebrew carpas, as Matthiolus writes from Aben-Ezra in his commentary on Dioscorides, book 3, chapter 69. Hence also petroselinum gets its name, as if it were "petra-selinon," that is, rock-parsley, for it grows among rocks.

Another color therefore is "carpasinus," namely green; another is "carbasinus" properly so called; for this is white, so named from "carbasus," which is linen of marvelous fineness, and therefore gleaming white, as witness
by another name "buccras," from the resemblance to horns # GEM

[On reclining at table] The body leaned on the left elbow, the lower parts stretched out lengthwise, lying on the couch with the head slightly raised and the back moderately supported by cushions. On each couch three or four reclined in such a manner that the feet of the one reclining in the uppermost place were extended near the one reclining in the second place, and the feet of the second near the one reclining in the third, and so on in order. Again, the head of the one reclining in the second place was turned toward the navel or breast of the one reclining in the first place; and in the same manner the rest reclined in succession. Hence we understand how Saint John at the Last Supper of Christ reclined upon His breast, as it were in His bosom; and how Saint Mary Magdalene, standing behind Jesus as He reclined at table, was able to wash His feet. Hence finally it is clear what triclinium signifies, namely a place of refreshment, in which commonly three couches were arranged around the table. Hence likewise these dining couches were called thori, stibadia, thoralia, accubitalia, which are found throughout in Cicero, Pliny, Athenaeus, Seneca, Virgil and others. See Giaconius, Fulgentius, Ursinus and others on the Triclinium.

[UPON THE PAVEMENT:] The Persians therefore dined and supped upon the pavement on the ground, after the manner of Orientals, just as Judith did, ch. 12 v. 19, as is clear from the Greek. For no other table is mentioned here.
[PARIAN:] that is, of white marble from the island of Paros, which is more excellent and precious than all others.
[PAINTING:] arising from a variety of stones, inlaid and woven together in work like mosaic. These stones are called in Hebrew dar and socharet; what they were is unknown.

AND THEY THAT WERE INVITED DRANK IN GOLDEN VESSELS; AND THE MEATS WERE BROUGHT IN DIFFERENT VESSELS ONE FROM ANOTHER.] The ancient Persians under Cyrus and before Cyrus were very frugal in food and drink; and, as Xenophon says in book 1 of the Cyropaedia, they had bread for food, cress for relish, and river water for drink. Whence Cicero in Tusculanae 1 says: the food of the Persians, he says, is set forth by Xenophon, who says they add nothing to their bread except cress. Hence they became hardy and strong for labors and wars, by which they obtained the kingdom and monarchy, which through the growth of gluttony and luxury they lost. On the temperance of the ancient Persians and the luxury of their successors, see Xenophon book 8; Stobaeus, sermon 114; Herodotus book 9; Athenaeus book 12.

Certainly Macrobius in the Saturnalia says that the table of the ancients was sacred, and that the ancients first remembered the divine power, then offered good wishes to their guests, and after prayer used food with moderation. Serinus in Stobaeus says that the kings of the Persians before eating debated about moderation, and before drinking about temperance. Indeed even Augustus offered banquets of only three courses.

What shall I speak of the adjurations of the powerful? What shall I recall of the oaths which they think it sacrilege to violate? "Let us drink," they say, "for the health of the Emperors; and he who shall not drink, let him be guilty of disloyalty; for he seems not to love the Emperor who has not drunk for his health." O what a service of filthy devotion! "Let us drink for the health of the armies, for the strength of the Comitatus, for our children." And they judge these vows to pertain to God, like those who carry cups to the tombs of the martyrs, and drink there in the evening, and believe they cannot otherwise be heard. O the folly of men, who think drunkenness a sacrifice; who suppose that those are pleased by drunkenness, who learned to sustain their passions by fasting! Is it not madness so to drink for another's health as to harm and destroy your own? Are they not rowers of cups and gluttons, who stuff their belly with food and wine like swine, nay more than swine? For a pig, however greedy, eats no more than to satisfy hunger, drinks no more than to quench thirst; nor does it allow itself to be driven by any blows or swords to do otherwise.

[APPOINTING OVER EACH TABLE ONE OF HIS PRINCES, THAT EVERY ONE MIGHT RECEIVE WHAT HE WISHED.] Among these princes was Zerubbabel, son of Salathiel, son of Jechoniah king of Judah, who together with his Hebrew companions, bodyguards of the king, was among the intimate courtiers of Darius, and after the banquet, disputing with them concerning who was strongest, carried off the palm. For when the first had said: "Wine is strong"; the second: "The king is stronger"; the third, Zerubbabel, said: "Women are stronger, but above all things truth conquers." Wherefore Darius, nodding his assent to him, said: "Ask what you wish, and I will give it to you." Then Zerubbabel said: "Remember your vow which you vowed to build Jerusalem on the day when you received the kingdom, and to send back all the vessels that were taken from Jerusalem. And you vowed to build the temple which the Edomites burned when Judea was laid waste by the Chaldeans. This is what I ask, that you fulfill the vow which you vowed to the King of heaven from your own mouth." Wherefore, when Darius had kissed him, he fulfilled the vow, and restored the temple, and sent back the vessels with gifts to Jerusalem, as is narrated at length elsewhere. # GEM

Allegorically, this banquet of Ahasuerus was literally a vain pursuit of empty pleasure and glory, but mystically it is a great image of the Holy Eucharist. For first, Ahasuerus the king and monarch represents Christ, King of kings and Lord of lords. Hence Ahasuerus in Hebrew and Chaldean means the same as "great head," "great king," and "prince." Lyranus adds: Ahasuerus is interpreted as "blessed"; but Christ is not only blessed, but also the beatitude of the saints. The interlinear Gloss indeed says: Ahasuerus shows forth Christ the Lord by name and dignity; for it is interpreted as "door." But Christ says: I am the door; if anyone enters through me, he will be saved. # GEM

Second, Ahasuerus celebrated this banquet in his royal palace of Susa, which word in Hebrew signifies "lily," the symbol of purity and virginity. So Christ in Susa, that is in the Church, which blooms with the lilies of the chaste and of virgins, instituted the Eucharist, which accordingly must be received by the chaste and most purely free from every stain; and thus received, it greatly increases purity and chastity. For it is itself the wheat of the elect, and the wine that makes virgins flourish, Zach. 9:17. Hence the Church, made not drunk but soberly joyful by this wine, exults, saying: As the dwelling of all who rejoice is in thee, Psalm 80:7. And: I will pass to the place of the wonderful tabernacle, even to the house of God, in the voice of exultation and confession, the sound of one feasting, Psalm 41:4. And: let the just be glad and rejoice in gladness, Psalm 67:4.

Third, Ahasuerus celebrated this banquet for seven days, that is, for the whole time of this age, which is conducted through seven days, says the interlinear Gloss, according to that saying of Christ, Matt. 28, last verse: Behold I am with you all days, even to the consummation of the world. So Lyranus. Fourth, on account of the greatness of the banquet. He made, it says, a great banquet. What is greater than the Eucharistic table, the Supper of the wayfarer par excellence? Hence the ordinary Gloss says: the history of this banquet shows forth the pomp of histories and the luxury of the King; but allegorically it signifies the spiritual delights of Christ, which he dispenses to each one.

Fifth, Ahasuerus invited not only the princes but also all the people from the least to the greatest: so Christ invites all to the Eucharist, both poor and rich, both women and men, both old and young. Sixth, on account of the magnificence of the banquet; for Lyranus and the Gloss assert that by the garden in which it takes place is signified the Church, which is an enclosed garden, planted with many trees, fruitful through holy life and fragrant through good reputation. By the awnings shading from above and the columns sustaining from below are indicated the intercessions of the Saints and the divine protections by which those who approach this table are defended. By the multiple colors of the hangings and cords — white, purple, hyacinth — are understood virgins, martyrs, and doctors, who are nourished by the feast of this banquet. By the golden and silver couches on which the guests reclined are expressed the tranquil and peaceful consciences which those who enjoy this divine food ought to have. By the gems and paintings which adorned the pavement with wondrous variety are intimated the virtues and other spiritual gifts with which the minds of those who communicate worthily are adorned.

Seventh, on account of the sweetness and delights of the food and drink: the wine, says Scripture, was set before them as was worthy of royal magnificence, and was of the finest. But in the Eucharist Christ is set forth as food, in whom, as in manna, we draw all the delight of Angels and all sweetness of spirit, according to that: He gave them bread from heaven; and man ate the bread of Angels, Psalm 77:24-25. # GEM

Queen Vashti also made a banquet for WOMEN. Hence aptly in Hebrew Vashti means the same as "drinking" or "warp-thread"; for threads and warp-threads are the work of women. For Vashti in Greek is Asti; in Josephus, Scesti; and from these are compounded the Persian names Astaspes and Hystaspes. Hence some scholars together with Serarius suspect that this Vashti was the daughter of Hystaspes and the sister of her husband Darius. Clesius favors this, asserting that the brother of Ahasuerus was Ondiaspes, which name alludes to Vashti, as if this Ondiaspis were her sister. White cotton curtains and blue hangings were fastened with cords of fine linen and purple - A description of extraordinary luxury and splendor in the decorations of the feast.

Drinks were served in golden vessels, vessels of different kinds - Each cup was unique and distinct, showing the incomparable wealth of the Persian king.

Moreover there is no doubt that Esther, a religious woman, abstained from foods forbidden her by the Law of Moses, such as things offered to idols, from which she abstained...
And seven most beautiful maidens] as companions and honorary attendants.

The twelfth month.] Therefore each virgin was adorned and prepared for a full year before she approached Ahasuerus; namely, for the first six months they were anointed with oil of myrrh, or that made from myrrh, and for the other six months with perfumes and delicate aromatics, and they used fragrances, especially those made from Heliocallis.

The use and origin of anointing is ascribed to the Persians: the ointment, he says, must belong to the Persian nation; they are steeped in it, and by this sought-after recommendation they extinguish the poison born of gluttony. The first instance I can find is that when the camp of King Darius was taken by storm, Alexander captured among the rest of his equipment a casket of ointments; afterwards this pleasure was admitted even among our own people as one of the most praised and honorable goods of life. Indeed Scripture also in Canticles 4 assigns ointments of myrrh, saffron, nard, etc. to the same people. So Lyra, Sanchez and others.

If Esther was thus anointed and adorned so as to please a mortal man, how much more was the Blessed Virgin anointed and adorned with grace and virtues, so that she might become the Spouse of God and Mother of Christ? Moreover, women are lovers of adornment and zealous in their ornamentation; hence the Comic poet says: What great business there is in a single woman? who night and day, at every age, are always adorning, washing, and polishing themselves. And again: While they are having their hair dressed, while they are being polished, a whole year passes. Athenaeus adds in book 1: Persian women are more devoted to adornment than is fitting.

SHE WAS THEREFORE LED TO THE BEDCHAMBER OF KING AHASUERUS IN THE TENTH MONTH (which corresponds partly to our December, partly to January), IN THE SEVENTH YEAR OF HIS REIGN. Therefore Esther waited for four years on account of the great number of virgins who were being brought in, before she herself was led to Ahasuerus; for in the third year of his reign, after repudiating Vashti, he took care to seek out other beautiful women, among whom was Esther.

Furthermore, these women, by the very fact that they were led to Ahasuerus, became his concubines, that is, secondary wives; from among whom the King then chose one principal wife as Queen, as he here chose Esther. For marriage was contracted by the mutual consent of the man and woman to the marital union.

Lapide asks whether the faithful Esther could lawfully marry the unbelieving Ahasuerus. He answers that she could, because Deut. 7:3 forbids the Jews only from intermarrying with Canaanites, not with Persians or other Gentile peoples. So hold Bellarmine, Serarius, Sanchez, and others. Add that Esther could not refuse the king's edict, lest she provoke his wrath against herself and the other Jews; indeed, had she resisted, the king could have abused her against her will and done her violence. Finally, Esther and Mordecai sensed a divine impulse to enter into marriage with the king, so that they might win him over to their whole people and thereby avert the danger of death threatening them from Haman.
Allegorically: Queen Esther is the Blessed Virgin Mary, as [Lapide] says. # GEM

into the chamber of the King in the Assumption, into the chamber of eternal rest, having grace above all women before the true King, that is, above all angelic intelligences and above all blessed souls, so that in Mary there is grace above the grace of all the blessed; for indeed the King of kings placed upon her head a diadem of the kingdom so beyond price, so delightful, so wonderful, that it is inexpressible by every tongue and inscrutable to every mind. Wherefore Methodius in his Hypapante teaches that the sacred head of Mary was crowned by God. Therefore Robert, book 3 on the Canticle, expounds concerning her: 'Thou shalt be crowned from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards': thou shalt be crowned, he says, that thou mayest be Queen of the Saints in heaven, and Queen of kingdoms on earth.

ANAGOGICAL SENSE: The Blessed Virgin, assumed into heaven, was there crowned Queen of the heavens, of men and of Angels. Hence in Apocalypse 12:1 she is called 'a woman clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars': see what is said there. Hear St. Bernard on the Assumption: # GEM Then the Blessed Virgin, like another Esther, led forth from the women's chamber, that is, from the Church Militant, and conducted through the whole host of the heavenly army to the chamber

The king loved her more than all women, because Esther surpassed all in beauty, modesty, bashfulness, purity, grace, and holiness; wherefore she was a type of the Blessed Virgin, whom the Angel greeted saying: Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with thee, blessed art thou among women (Luke 1).

And he gave rest to all the provinces — that is, from tributes and other burdens, relaxing taxes which he had previously imposed upon the people. For Herodotus (bk. 3) teaches that Darius imposed many such things, saying: Under Cyrus and then under Cambyses nothing had yet been established concerning tributes, but gifts were brought. On account of this ordering of tribute and other such matters, the Persians say Darius was a shopkeeper, Cambyses a master, and Cyrus a father: because Darius made gain of all things, Cambyses was harsh and morose, but Cyrus acting mildly strove by every means to deserve well of them. Others understand this rest as relief from labors, meaning that Darius commanded all to rest from their works and to keep festival days in joy and banquets on account of such distinguished nuptials contracted with Esther. "And he gave gifts according to princely magnificence" — that is, royal; for the Hebrew reads according to the magnificence of the king, namely such as befitted so great a king as Darius was.

And when virgins were sought a second time. From this it appears that virgins were sought twice for Ahasuerus, which is what these words signify. For polygamy was then customary, especially among kings. Hence Solomon had 700 queens and 300 concubines, that is, secondary wives. So even now the kings of the Turks, Arabs, and Indians maintain a great multitude of wives. For lust is insatiable, as well as fastidious and curious; for those whom they have once known they soon hold in contempt, and seek others, not so much to satisfy their licentiousness as to vary it, and to discover whether greater pleasure may be found elsewhere.

She did all things as she had been accustomed when he brought her up as a little child. Note here the rare obedience of Esther, who, now made queen and indeed empress, nevertheless obeyed Mordecai as she had obeyed him when a little child; and through this obedience God most wisely directed her in all things, so that she might overthrow Haman and deliver her people from death.

Bigthan and Teresh, two eunuchs of the palace and courtiers of Darius, were angry and wished to rise up against the king and kill him. What was their cause? Various authors assign various reasons with some probability. The Septuagint gives this reason: that the king had preferred Mordecai over them. Others say it was because he had repudiated Vashti and substituted Esther in her place. Others say it was because they were not content with their own dignity and rank but sought a greater position, even the kingdom itself, just as Smerdis the Magian had previously seized the kingdom by deceit before Darius. Others, more plausibly, think they wished to transfer the kingdom to Haman, who was first after the king; and therefore they hold Haman to have been privy to their conspiracy, or indeed its instigator, concluding this from...

Aman had been hostile to Mordecai, because Mordecai himself had been the cause that those two eunuchs were hanged. Those men were therefore friends, supporters, and agents of Aman, so that with the king slain through them, he might seize that position as if first in the king's favor; and because of this, the king said...

Post haec rex Assuerus exaltavit Aman filium Amadathi: After these things, Ahasuerus elevated Haman the son of Hammedatha. Josephus son of Gorion writes that when Mordecai sat at the king's gate, he discovered the conspiracy of two eunuchs of the king who guarded the door and plotted to raise their hands against the king and carry his head to the king of Macedonia, who had already declared war against the kings of Persia. When these eunuchs were hanged on the cross, Haman was exceedingly angry that his counselors had been so dealt with by crucifixion, and attempted to exterminate all the Jews. # GEM

Of this war declared by the king of Macedonia against Darius and Persia we read nothing elsewhere; yet there could have been some suspicion of it, both because shortly afterwards Xerxes son of Darius waged war against the Macedonians and Greeks, and because Alexander the Great, who was king of Macedonia, after some years transferred the monarchy from Darius Codomannus and the Persians to himself and the Macedonians, and also because Haman, who was a Macedonian, was ambitious for the kingdom, of which more at ch. 3 v. 1.

SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER THREE: AHASUERUS EXALTS HAMAN AND COMMANDS ALL TO WORSHIP HIM. MORDECAI REFUSES TO DO SO. WHEREUPON HAMAN OBTAINS FROM AHASUERUS A DECREE THAT HE HIMSELF AND ALL THE JEWS SHALL BE KILLED ON AN APPOINTED DAY.

Post haec rex Assuerus exaltavit Aman filium Amadathi qui erat de stirpe Agag: After these things king Ahasuerus exalted Haman the son of Hammedatha who was of the seed of Agag, and set his seat above all the princes he had.

Cunctique servi regis qui in foribus palatii versabantur flectebant genua et adorabant Aman: All the king's servants who were at the palace gate bowed the knee and worshipped Haman, for so the emperor had commanded them; Mordecai alone did not bow the knee nor worship him.
Cui dixerunt pueri regis qui ad fores palatii praesidebant: The king's servants who presided at the palace gates said to him: Why do you, contrary to all others, not observe the king's command?

Cumque hoc crebrius dicerent et ille nollet audire: When they said this frequently and he would not hear, they told Haman, wishing to know whether he would persist in his resolution, for he had told them that he was a Jew.

Quod cum audisset Aman et experimento probasse quod Mardochaeus non flecteret sibi genu: When Haman had heard this and had proved by experience that Mordecai did not bow the knee to him nor worship him, he was exceedingly angry.

Et pro nihilo duxit in unum Mardochaeum mittere manus suas: And he thought it beneath him to lay hands on Mordecai alone, for he had heard that he was of the Jewish people, and preferred rather to destroy the whole nation of the Jews throughout the kingdom of Ahasuerus.

Mense primo cujus vocabulum est Nisan anno duodecimo regni Assueri missa est sors in urnam quae Hebraice dicitur phur: In the first month, whose name is Nisan, in the twelfth year of king Ahasuerus, the lot was cast into the urn, which in Hebrew is called Pur, before Haman, to determine on what day and in what month the Jewish people ought to be destroyed; and the twelfth month, which is called Adar, came forth.

Dixitque Aman regi Assuero: Est populus per omnes provincias regni tui dispersus: Haman said to king Ahasuerus: There is a people scattered throughout all the provinces of your kingdom and separated from one another, using new laws and ceremonies, and moreover despising the king's statutes. And you know very well that it is not expedient for your kingdom that they should grow insolent through license.

Si tibi placet decerne ut pereat et decem millia talentorum appendam arcariis gazae tuae: If it please you, decree that they shall be destroyed, and I will pay ten thousand talents of silver into the king's treasury.

Tulit ergo rex annulum quo utebatur de manu sua et dedit eum Aman filio Amadathi de progenie Agag hosti Judaeorum: The king therefore took the ring which he wore from his hand and gave it to Haman the son of Hammedatha of the race of Agag, the enemy of the Jews.

Dixitque ad eum: Argentum quod tu polliceris tuum sit de populo age quod tibi placet: And said to him: The silver which you promise shall be your own; as for the people, do with them as it pleases you.

Vocatique sunt scribae regis mense primo Nisan tertia decima die ejusdem mensis: The king's scribes were called in the first month Nisan on the thirteenth day of the same month; and it was written as Haman had commanded to all the king's satraps and judges of the provinces and of diverse nations, so that each nation could read and hear in the variety of their languages, in the name of king Ahasuerus, and the letters were sealed with his ring.

Missae sunt per cursores regis ad universas provincias ut occiderent atque delerent omnes Judaeos a puero usque ad senem parvulos et mulieres uno die: They were sent by the king's couriers to all the provinces, to kill and destroy all the Jews, from the young to the old, children and women, in one day, that is the thirteenth of the twelfth month which is called Adar, and to plunder their goods.
Summa autem epistolarum haec fuit ut omnes: The sum of the letters was this, that all peoples should be made ready for that day.

Concerning Haman's origin: he was by race an Amalekite but by country a Macedonian. His parents were Amalekites, but after the Amalekites were destroyed partly by Saul and partly by Nebuchadnezzar, Haman's parents fled as refugees to other nations, and through wanderings and misfortunes came to Macedonia, where they settled and begat Haman.

A Chaldean manuscript adds that Haman had been a slave, and therefore when elevated by Ahasuerus he became puffed up and intolerable. For as it is said in Proverbs 30:21-22: 'For three things the earth is moved, and the fourth it cannot bear: for a slave when he reigns,' etc. # GEM

The Chaldean adds that Haman was that very man [mentioned elsewhere], noting that those of servile origin, when raised to power, become insufferable in their arrogance, unable to bear the dignity of their position with moderation.

Ahasuerus repudiated Vashti and substituted his own daughter in her place; but there befell his daughter so great and so foul a flux of the bowels that by her stench she drove everyone away. Moreover, Haman had gone up to Jerusalem to hinder the building of the Temple, and many similar things of this kind, which seem to be inventions and fictions of the Rabbis.

And all the king's servants... bowed the knee and worshipped Haman; for so the emperor had commanded them. See here how greatly Ahasuerus had exalted Haman, that he commanded him to be adored as some god and divine power. For the kings of the Persians wished to be adored as gods and divine powers. Hence just as Jupiter in heaven is said to create lesser gods, so Ahasuerus created Haman as a lesser god on earth, next to himself, and therefore to be adored by all: so says Serarius, of whom more presently.

But Mordecai bowed not the knee, neither did he worship him. Why did Mordecai refuse to bow the knee before Haman? First, a Chaldean manuscript answers, on account of the unworthiness of the matter, in that Haman had been a servant of Mordecai; but this is uncertain and hardly probable, nor is it sufficient; indeed Mordecai himself refutes it, saying in chapter 13:13: "I would have been ready to kiss the soles of his feet."
Secondly, the Jews give the reason that Haman wore on his garment painted images of idols; but this is equally uncertain and improbable.

Thirdly, Cajetan offers another reason, namely that Haman was an Amalekite of the stock of Agag; and God had commanded that the Amalekites be destroyed by the Hebrews, Deut. 24. But that hatred pertained to the extermination of the nation of Amalek, not to refusing honour to one Haman in Persia at such great peril to Mordecai and the Hebrews.

Fourthly, the same Cajetan says: the Jews bowed one knee to a prince, both knees to God; but Haman wished both knees bent to himself, and therefore Mordecai refused. But this seems false and fabricated; for Abraham bowing both knees, indeed prostrating himself on the ground, worshipped the sons of Heth, as I said on Genesis 23:7.

Fifthly, Gabriel Vasquez, On Adoration, book 1, disputation 5, chapter 3, considers that Mordecai was unwilling to bow the knee and worship Haman because he himself daily bowed the knee and worshipped God; because this daily genuflection was reserved according to the custom of the Jews. But Haman, entering the royal court daily, daily demanded this genuflection from Mordecai and the courtiers. Yet Mordecai was unwilling even once to genuflect and worship Haman. For if it had been lawful once, it would certainly have been lawful daily.

# GEM I say therefore: Mordecai himself assigns the true cause in chapter 13, saying: "But I feared lest I should transfer the honour of my God to a man, and lest I should adore any one except my God." The same

Ahasuerus had made Aman equal to himself, and had therefore commanded that he be adored properly as a God. For the kings of the Persians demanded from their subjects divine honor and adoration due to God, as is clear from Justin book 6, and Herodotus book 7, where he says the Lacedaemonians, though ordered, refused to adore Xerxes because he was a Persian. In Plutarch on Artabanus: "The most beautiful law among us," they say, "is to honor the king and adore the image of the God who preserves all things." Q. Curtius on Darius: "The king," he says, "had just been conveyed in a chariot, and was worshipped by his subjects with the honors of the Gods." Hence Aeschylus said the Persians held kings in the place of Gods. Xenophon asserts the same of the Persians in his Oration on Agesilaus, as does Aristotle in book 3 of the Rhetoric, Isocrates in the Panegyric, Strabo book 10, Heliodorus books 7 and 9, Seneca book 3 On Benefits chapter 12, Herodian book 1, and Lampridius in the Life of Alexander Severus, so that there is no doubt on this matter.

Hence, as Curtius testifies in book 8, when Cleon the Sicilian was persuading Alexander to assume a divine name for himself, he adduced the example of the Persians whom he had already conquered; for he said the Persians not only piously but also prudently worshipped their kings among the Gods, for the majesty of empire is the guardian of safety. And when the Macedonians could not bear the arrogance of Alexander, who had already decreed himself to be among the number of the Gods, and some of them had conspired against his life, one of them when caught used the excuse that his arrogance did not seem tolerable. This was Hermolaus, who said: "Delighted by Persian dress and discipline, you have come to loathe ancestral customs; it was therefore a Persian king, not a Macedonian one, we wished to kill, and we pursue you as a deserter by the law of war. You wanted the Macedonians to bow the knee to you and to venerate you as a God. You turn away from Philip your father, and if anyone were held before Jupiter among the Gods, you would disdain even Jupiter. Do you marvel that free men cannot bear your arrogance?" Thus Curtius.

In a similar manner Nebuchadnezzar wished to be adored as a God in his statue, and the three companions of Daniel who refused were cast by him into the burning furnace but were preserved unharmed by God, Daniel chapter 2. And the other Nebuchadnezzar commanded Holofernes everywhere to remove all Gods, so that he alone might be worshipped as if God, Judith 3:13. Thus Virgil sings of Augustus Caesar in the first Eclogue: "For he shall always be a God to me; his altar a tender lamb from our folds shall often stain." Thus also Caligula wished to be adored as a God; and Domitian, to whom therefore Martial in flattery says: "The edict of our Lord and God."

Hence also Persian adoration was performed with this rite, which savored of divinity. For those adoring would genuflect, and prostrating themselves face-down would touch their forehead to the ground and kiss the earth, as Curtius testifies in books 5 and 8, and Valerius Maximus book 7 chapter 3, Josephus Antiquities 20 chapter 3, and Martial book 10 Epigram 72, who accordingly blame and mock it as too arrogant and redolent of divinity. See concerning the rite of adoration of the Persian kings, Brisson book 1 On the Persian Kingdom, page 10 and following, where he also narrates that Pelopidas and other Greeks refused to adore Artaxerxes with this rite; indeed that the Athenians punished Themagoras with death because he had adored Darius in this manner, as Valerius Maximus book 6 relates.

One might say: Mordecai could have dissembled the intention of Aman and simply adore him with civil and political adoration, namely to bend the knee to him and prostrate himself on the ground as to a prince, not as to a God; for these external gestures are indifferent and are distinguished by the intention of the one performing them, so that if he has the intention of one truly adoring, they are deemed gestures of adoration and latria, but if only of one honoring, they are deemed gestures of reverence and political honor. So argues Salianus, who therefore thinks Mordecai, Ezra, and Nehemiah sometimes rendered these gestures of adoration to the Persian kings not as to Gods but as to princes. The answer is that he could not do this, because the king had publicly commanded that Aman be adored as a God, and Aman wished to be adored as such, and this expressly. # GEM
Mordecai had worshipped Holofernes as a prince, not as a God; because Holofernes declared himself to be not God, but only Nabuchodonosor's representative.

Some add that Mordecai was of more delicate, timid, and scrupulous conscience, so that he would admit not even any shadow of idolatry, nor any sign by which even the least honor of God might seem to be injured and a neighbor offended. Hence he himself says in ch. 13: "I feared lest I should transfer the honor of my God to a man."

# GEM He had told them that he was a Jew, and therefore was forbidden by Jewish religion and law to adore any man, but only God alone, since the Persians and other pagans adored even men, namely their kings, as though they were gods. It is probable that Esdras, Nehemiah, Esther, Zorobabel, and the rest of the Hebrews who lived at their courts, indeed who held honored offices, answered the same to Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes — namely that they were Jews, to whom it is not lawful to adore any other than God — and therefore that these kings did not exact from them, as Jews, the Persian adoration, but only the political and civil reverence which is customarily shown to princes as men.

Add to this that these kings exacted that Persian adoration only in public, when they displayed themselves to the people as beings worthy of veneration as divinities; for in private from their courtiers and chamberlains, with whom they dealt familiarly day by day, they did not demand it, being content with common honor and reverence. For to render the Persian adoration at each individual address and service would have been an intolerable burden and morally impossible.

# GEM "And he desired rather to destroy all the nation of the Jews that were in the kingdom of Assuerus." The word "rather" implies that Aman had previously wished to destroy Mordecai and the Jews, because Mordecai had accused his two eunuch friends, so that they were led to the cross.

MISSA EST SORS IN URNAM, QUAE HEBRAICE DICITUR PHUR: For "lot" in Hebrew is גּוֹרָל goral. Hence Lyra, Cajetan, Marin, and others think that phur is a Persian word, while goral is Hebrew: that the Hebrews received the word phur from the Persians in this passage, and therefore called this Persian lot by their name phur. Hence the Hebrews have it thus: "A phur was cast; it is the goral," that is, the lot. Hence also the word phur is found nowhere in the Bible except in this book of Esther. For in the Psalms, Proverbs, and other books, the Hebrew word for lot is always goral, not phur. Some however think that phur is also a Hebrew word, from the root phar, meaning "to fall," because the lot falls and drops into the urn.

Moreover, from phur, that is "lot," the feast day was called by the Hebrews Purim, on which they gave thanks to God because He had delivered them from the evil lot of destruction cast by Haman. Haman employed the lot here out of Gentile superstition, as those who used the lot as a kind of divination to search out hidden and dangerous things, as was done with Jonah, ch. 1.
column4: # GEM For, as St. Augustine says on Psalm 30: "A lot is a thing in human doubt, indicating the divine will." Hence Cicero, book 1 On Divination, calls lots things "which are poured forth by divination." Virgil, Aeneid book 4: "The Lycian lots bade me seek Italy" — that is, oracles. Ovid, Metamorphoses book 1: "It pleased them to implore the heavenly power and seek aid through sacred lots."

column3: But this superstition deceived Haman; for it postponed the time of the slaughter of the Jews, and thus gave Esther space to plead against the slaughter. For if he had executed the matter immediately upon receiving the king's permission, he would have killed all the Jews. His delay and casting of lots was therefore imprudent; truly God frustrated his cruel counsel. The sense is therefore: Haman cast a lot, so that from it, as from divination and oracle, he might learn which day would be fitting for the slaughter of the Jews and fortunate and auspicious for himself, lest he should incur any danger to his life or his people from the Jews or their defenders.

SI TIBI PLACET, DECERNE UT PEREAT, ET DECEM MILLIA TALENTORUM (of silver, as the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Septuagint have it) APPENDAM ARCIS GAZAE TUAE: that is, five million gold pieces, if Attic talents are understood here, as seems to be the case; but if Hebrew talents, the sum will be double, namely ten million gold pieces. This is an immense price, but it was easy for Haman to collect it from the goods of the Jews already destined by him for plunder and spoliation. Haman offered this to Darius because he knew that he, being greedy for gold, would be greatly moved by the large sum offered.

On the pride and cruelty of Haman: he wished to kill all the Jews, who were entirely innocent, on account of one man Mordecai alone. "Mordecai alone, O Haman, offended you; pursue him alone therefore, if you wish: why do you vomit your wrath upon all the Jews, who have not offended you?" Second, on the great dignity of Mordecai: that on his account Haman decreed to destroy the entire race of the Jews, because Mordecai was their choragus, as it were their leader and prince, so that they were governed by him, and what he did the rest would do, following his example.

Mardochaeus, hearing the cruel edict for the destruction of the Jews, rent his garments and put on sackcloth, sprinkling ashes upon his head, and went crying with a loud voice through the midst of the city, showing the bitterness of his soul.
He went with this lamentation even to the gates of the palace; for it was not lawful to enter the king's court clothed in sackcloth.
In all the provinces, towns, and places to which the king's cruel decree had come, there was great mourning among the Jews, fasting, wailing and weeping, with many lying upon sackcloth and ashes.
The maidens and eunuchs came in and told Esther; and when she heard it she was exceedingly troubled, and sent garments to Mardochaeus to clothe him and take away his sackcloth; but he refused them.
Then she called Athach the eunuch whom the king had appointed to serve her, and commanded him to go to Mardochaeus and learn from him why he was doing this.
Athach went out and found Mardochaeus standing in the street of the city before the gate of the palace.
He told him all that had happened, how Aman had promised to pay money into the king's treasury for the slaughter of the Jews.
He also gave him a copy of the edict that had been posted in Susa, to show to the queen, and to admonish her to go in to the king and entreat him for her people.
Athach returned and told Esther all that Mardochaeus had said.
She answered him and commanded him to say to Mardochaeus:

All the king's servants and all the provinces under his dominion know that whosoever, whether man or woman, shall come to the king into the inner court without being called, there is one law: that he be put to death, except the king shall hold out the golden sceptre to him as a sign of life. How then can I go in to the king, who have not been called these thirty days?

# GEM Lapide notes that Mardochaeus rent his garments as a sign of extreme grief, for among the Hebrews the tearing of garments signified not ordinary sorrow but anguish of soul so vehement that it could not be contained within, and so burst forth outwardly. So Jacob rent his garments at the reported death of Joseph (Gen. 37), as did Joshua and Caleb (Num. 14), David at the death of Saul (2 Sam. 1), and Job when struck by calamity (Job 1). This external act of grief was thus both natural and divinely permitted among God's people.

Lapide notes: it was not permitted to enter the royal court clothed in sackcloth, lest the king's presence be saddened by signs of mourning. Persian kings by ancient custom required all who approached them to appear in festive and cheerful garb. This same prohibition existed in Rome, where mourning dress was forbidden in the Forum on certain solemn days.

Lapide observes that fasting, sackcloth and ashes were the three principal instruments of Jewish public penance and supplication, signifying the mortification of the body and its desires, and the humbling of the soul before God. By these means they sought to move God to mercy, as Nineveh moved Him (Jonah 3), and as Joel commanded (Joel 2:12).

Lapide here remarks on the enormous wickedness of Aman, who sought not only the blood of innocent people but also made a profit from it, promising ten thousand talents of silver to the king's treasury for the license to destroy the Jews. So wickedness compounds itself: cruelty united with avarice produces the most monstrous crimes.

# GEM Lapide notes that Mardochaeus wisely sent to Esther a copy of the edict itself, so that she might see with her own eyes the full horror of what threatened her people, and be moved not merely by report but by the very words of the decree to intercede with the king. This is a lesson in the art of persuasion: those who seek to move others to action must set before them the concrete reality of the danger, not merely speak of it in general terms.

Lapide explains the Persian law here cited: it was a capital offense to approach the king unsummoned, even for queens and the highest nobles, lest the king be disturbed or assassination be facilitated. Herodotus, Diodorus and others record this custom among the Persians and Medes. The golden sceptre held out by the king was the sign of his favour and the guarantee of the petitioner's life. Esther had not been called for thirty days, which suggested she had perhaps fallen from favour, making her approach all the more perilous.

In the square in the middle of the city — that is, in the forum or similar public place, where all congregated. For this reason a forum is customarily situated in the middle of a city, so that it may be easily approached by citizens dwelling on all sides, for buying and selling necessary goods.

He cried with a loud voice — What he cried out the Greek text explains, namely that innocent Jews who had sinned nothing were condemned to death. This custom of crying out in great calamities was common and frequent among the Persians, as Curtius (books 4 and 5) and Herodotus (books 3 and 8) attest, so that they might move all to pity and arouse someone who might bring or suggest a remedy for the evil.
The cruel decree of the king — that is, the edict. Hence in Hebrew it is "the Word of the King"; thus 'dogma' is taken for a decree in Acts 16:4, and by Josephus, Demosthenes, and others.

The inner court of the king — in which the king sat upon a golden throne, gleaming with gold and gems, like some earthly god. Moreover there was a law that if anyone should enter this court uninvited, he should be killed immediately without any hesitation. The reason for this law, says Rupert (book 8, On the Victory of the Word [of God]),

#GEM St. Ambrose pursues this same point: She liberated her entire race, that is, the whole people of the Jews, from the bitterness of persecution; so that she made the king subject to herself, not inflamed by the love of lust, but turned by heavenly compassion; so that punishment was turned back upon the impious, and honor was restored to the sacred altars. And so she who fasted continuously for three days, and washed her body with water, pleased him more and obtained vengeance. But Haman, while he boasted of the royal banquet, amid the very wines paid the penalty of his drunkenness. Fasting therefore is the sacrifice of reconciliation, the increase of virtue, which made even women stronger by the increase of grace.

#GEM And Clement of Alexandria (Stromata, bk. 6, ch. 11) says: Again, that Esther with perfect faith, liberating Israel from tyrannical power and from the cruelty of the Satraps, a single woman afflicted with fasts, resisted armed forces innumerable, dissolving through faith the tyrannical decree; and she indeed softened the tyrant, but repressed Haman, and by perfect prayer to God preserved Israel unharmed.
Allegorically: Esther represents the Church; whence...

And he extended against her the golden rod,] that is, the golden scepter which he carried in his hand, by this extension signifying his clemency and benevolence toward her, so that she might safely approach him, though called against the law.

Allegor. The golden rod is the Blessed Virgin, through whom God, offended at the whole human race, was reconciled to the same; of whom Balaam prophesied, Num. 24 near the end, saying: A star (the Blessed Virgin, who is the true Esther and queen) shall rise out of Jacob, and a rod shall spring up from Israel, and shall smite the princes of Moab, and shall waste all the children of Seth. # GEM This is, says Blessed Peter Damian, serm. on the Assumption, that rod by which the assaults of opposing demons are beaten back, the rod of Aaron through which wonders are wrought. But understand the staff of the Cross, by which not only was he beaten but slain, that insatiable murderer who feeds and nourishes himself on the deaths of men. In the Virgin as rod and in the Cross as staff is contained the hope and consolation of the wretched, as the sublime Prophet declares with clear voice: Thy rod, he says, and thy staff, these have comforted me.

Even if thou hadst asked for half of my kingdom, thou wouldst obtain it.] God was more liberal to the Blessed Virgin than Ahasuerus, for he granted her the whole of his kingdom, namely the dominion of heaven. Hence the Angels as if to their queen sang at Rome at the Castel Sant'Angelo: Queen of heaven, rejoice, alleluia, as I have related more fully elsewhere. For to her as to his mother Christ can deny nothing; wherefore the greatest benefit of God to men after the Incarnate Word is the Blessed Virgin; for he gave her to us so that through her we might obtain all things from him, so that she herself might open to us all the treasures of his clemency, omnipotence, and divinity, and bestow all the graces hidden therein through her Son.

Let the king and Haman come to the banquet which I have prepared for them.] The Greek adds that Esther gave the reason for the invitation, saying: My day is notable today; as if to say: Today is a feast for me, and therefore there must be rejoicing and a banquet; I therefore invite the king, that he may honor it with his presence. Esther prudently invited the king a second time to a banquet, so that therein she might lay before the king her petition for the salvation of the Jews. First, so that she might gradually inflame the king more in love for her, so that he could deny her nothing. For it was a difficult and very hard thing to ask of the king that he should revoke his edict concerning the slaughter of the Jews, already issued and proclaimed, contrary to the custom of the kings of the Persians and Medes. Second, because she did not dare to ask this publicly in so great a crowd of assembled nobles, since they would have said that a law enacted according to royal custom could not be revoked. Secretly therefore at the banquet she asked this of the king and obtained it; secretly revealing that she was a Jewess, which to have done publicly before the princes would have been dangerous. Third, she invited Haman alone with the king, so as to bring him into the envy and hatred of the remaining satraps, says Lyra. Fourth, because she knew that at a banquet the king, cheered by more generous drink, would be more liberal in granting requests. So even now prudent men, when they seek something from someone more harsh, do not ask it in the morning when the mind and countenance are more austere from fasting, but after dinner and a feast, when they have made him merry with wine, they demand and obtain it. Some add that it was the custom of Persian kings to deny nothing to anyone at a banquet. Certainly Herodotus in that book writes that among the Persians it was unlawful for the king…
[On Esther's approach to the king] She prepared a banquet, wishing to soften the king's mind gradually rather than make her petition at once.

[On Mordecai sitting before the palace gates] This was of great constancy in Mordecai, that he did not rise even for Haman, that most wicked enemy who had plotted the exile of the whole Jewish people, so as to show his trust in God, and by his very action to reproach Haman for his pride and cruelty.
And though I have all these things, I think I have nothing, so long as I see Mordecai.
column4: Mordecai torments you, O Haman, more than you torment him: you have nothing therefore without Mordecai, because Mordecai has everything, and therefore you have nothing. Rightly does St. Bernard exclaim, lib. 1 de consideratione: "Ambition is the cross of the ambitious—how does it please all while tormenting all? Nothing more bitterly torments, nothing more troublesomely disturbs, and yet among wretched mortals nothing is more celebrated than its pursuits"; and in sermon 60 de Ascensione: "This is the mountain to which the angel ascended and became the devil." # GEM Wittily does St. Anselm, lib. de similitudinibus c. 77, compare honor to a butterfly: "Thus act those who seek the honors of this world, as boys who chase a butterfly. For butterflies, when they fly, never keep a straight path, but dart here and there; when they seem to settle somewhere, they do not stay long. The boys chasing them, eager to catch them, run quickly after them, and because they attend not to their own feet but to the butterflies, they sometimes fall into a pit. Often when they approach carefully and try to seize them, the butterflies fly away; when they do catch them, they exult over nothing, as though they had obtained something great. Similarly act those who seek the honors of this world. For the honors of this world never hold a fixed course, but flit from one to another by uncertain byways."

column3: He ordered a lofty gibbet to be prepared.
column4: But the cross you prepared for Mordecai, O Haman, you fixed for yourself. For upon it you yourself will shortly be hanged, according to that saying of Ecclesiasticus 27:29: "He that diggeth a pit shall fall into it, and he that setteth a stone for his neighbor shall stumble upon it; and he that layeth a snare for another shall perish in it." See what is said there. Moreover he ordered a cross rather than a pyre, sword, or other torment to be prepared, because the cross was customary among the Persians and was most ignominious. Another reason, derived from the Rabbis, is given by the Chaldean Manuscript, which hear: "Zeresh his wife said to Haman her husband: You cannot cast Mordecai into fire, because Abraham his father was delivered from it. You cannot drown him in water, because Moses and the children of Israel were delivered through it. You cannot cast him into a lion's den, because Daniel the Prophet was delivered from it. But let them make a gibbet in an open place fifty cubits high, and in the morning speak to the king that Mordecai be hanged upon it; for in this case there has never been proved one of those who was delivered when hanged upon the wood of a gibbet." But these are the usual fictions of the Rabbinical imagination.

column3: NOCTEM ILLAM DUXIT REX INSOMNEM.] God, directing this through an Angel, arranged that Ahasuerus should read his annals, and thereby recall the benefit rendered to him by Mordecai, and honor him: and at the same time, by the example of Ahasuerus, to teach morally that kings and princes ought not to sleep whole nights in bed, but to be vigilant over the greatest public affairs of the state, and to beseech God for them. Well known is that saying of Homer: "It is not fitting for a counselor-man to sleep the whole night through." # GEM That institution, says Brisonius in Book 1 on the Kingdom of the Persians, which flourished among the Persians and was never sufficiently praised, was this: as Plutarch relates in his book To an Uneducated Prince, that daily one of the chamberlains appointed for that purpose, entering the king's chamber in the morning, would cry out: "Arise, O King, and attend to the affairs which Mesoromasdes willed you to attend to." Now Mesoromasdes, as Laertius says at the beginning, is the Sun, which was called Mithra by them. Constantine the Great, says Eusebius in Book 4 of his Life

VENTUM EST AD ILLUM LOCUM UBI SCRIPTUM ERAT.] God in His providence so arranged the reading of the annals that it came precisely to the passage recording how Mordecai had revealed the conspiracy of Bigthana and Teresh, the eunuchs who had sought to kill King Ahasuerus. Thus divine providence works through seemingly chance occurrences to accomplish its purposes, rewarding the faithful and overthrowing the proud.

QUID PRO HAC FIDE HONORIS AC PRAEMII MARDOCHAEUS CONSECUTUS EST?] The king asks what honor and reward Mordecai received for this loyalty. His servants reply that he received nothing at all. # GEM Here we see that earthly kings often forget benefits done to them, and defer rewards until God Himself intervenes to remind them. Seneca notes in De Beneficiis that ingratitude is the most shameful of vices: yet God permits such forgetfulness in princes so that He may Himself at the appointed time restore justice and reward the faithful. Mordecai had saved the king's life and received nothing; yet now, at the very moment when Haman seeks his death, God moves the king's heart to honor him. Thus the ways of divine providence are wonderful.

STATIMQUE REX: QUIS EST IN ATRIO?] The king immediately asks who is in the court. For God had so arranged things that Haman had entered the inner court of the royal palace at precisely this moment, intending to ask the king that Mordecai be hanged on the gallows he had prepared for him. Thus God's timing is perfect: at the very moment Haman comes to seek Mordecai's death, God moves the king to seek Mordecai's honor. The enemy comes to destroy, but God turns his coming to the salvation of the just.

AMAN STAT IN ATRIO. DIXITQUE REX: INGREDIATUR.] The servants report that Haman is standing in the court, and the king commands him to enter. Thus Haman, who came with murder in his heart, is summoned into the king's presence, where he will become the unwilling instrument of the very honor he most sought to deny.

QUID DEBET FIERI VIRO QUEM REX HONORARE DESIDERAT?] The king asks Haman what should be done for the man whom the king wishes to honor. Haman, thinking in his heart that the king wished to honor none other than himself, prepares to answer with the greatest honors he can imagine. # GEM Note here the pride and blindness of Haman. Pride blinds the mind, so that a man sees everything through the lens of his own self-love. Haman never considered that another might be more worthy of royal honor than himself. Thus pride is the root of all folly, as Bernard says: the proud man attributes to himself what belongs to God and to others.

RESPONDIT: HOMO QUEM REX HONORARE CUPIT.] Haman begins his answer, proposing honors fit for a king rather than a subject, thereby revealing the ambition secretly harbored in his heart. # GEM Chrysostom observes that nothing so clearly betrays the hidden desires of the heart as the counsel a man gives when he believes he is speaking on his own behalf. Haman here unwittingly confesses his secret aspiration to royal dignity.

DEBET INDUI VESTIBUS REGIIS.] Haman proposes that the man to be honored should be clothed in royal garments, placed upon the king's own horse, and receive the royal diadem upon his head. These three honors — royal robes, the king's horse, and the royal crown — represent the fullness of royal dignity. Haman desires all three for himself, not knowing that by proposing them he is preparing them for his enemy Mordecai.

ET PRIMUS DE REGIS PRINCIPIBUS AC TYRANNIS TENEAT EQUUM EJUS.] Furthermore, Haman proposes that one of the king's chief princes should lead the horse through the city square, crying out: "Thus shall it be done to the man whom the king wishes to honor." How bitter this will be for Haman himself to perform! He who wished to be led in triumph now must lead another. # GEM Augustine notes in the City of God that pride receives no more fitting punishment than to be compelled to serve those whom it despised.

FAC UT LOCUTUS ES MARDOCHAEO JUDAEO QUI SEDET ANTE FORES PALATII.] The king commands Haman to do immediately for Mordecai the Jew all that he has proposed, omitting nothing. The divine irony is complete: Haman the enemy of the Jews must publicly honor Mordecai the Jew, the very man he came to destroy. God thus confounds the proud in their own devices, as the Psalmist says: "He has made a pit and dug it, and has fallen into the ditch which he made" (Ps. 7:16). # GEM Gregory the Great observes that the proud are most fittingly punished by being made instruments of the glory of those they persecuted.

TULIT ITAQUE AMAN STOLAM ET EQUUM.] So Haman took the robe and the horse, clothed Mordecai, set him upon the horse, and went before him through the city square, crying: "Thus shall be done to the man whom the king wishes to honor."
