Every step of this procession was torment for Haman: he who had prepared the gallows for Mordecai now prepares his triumph; he who had sought his enemy's death now proclaims his glory before all the city. Such is the justice of God, which turns the counsels of the wicked back upon their own heads.

REVERSUSQUE EST MARDOCHAEUS AD JANUAM PALATII ET AMAN FESTINAVIT IRE IN DOMUM SUAM LUGENS ET OPERTO CAPITE.] Mordecai returned to the palace gate, while Haman hurried home in mourning with his head covered. Note the contrast: Mordecai, who had been sitting in sackcloth and ashes, now returns to his post with honor; Haman, who had come in pride, departs in shame. The covered head was a sign of grief and confusion among the Persians, as it was also among the Romans, as Valerius Maximus records. # GEM Bernard says: "The higher pride raises a man, the deeper humiliation throws him down; and the fall is all the more shameful the higher the pride that preceded it."

SI DE SEMINE JUDAEORUM EST MARDOCHAEUS ANTE QUEM CADERE COEPISTI NON POTERIS EI RESISTERE SED CADES IN CONSPECTU EJUS.] Haman's own wife Zeresh and his wise counselors, seeing the beginning of his downfall, declare that if Mordecai is of Jewish seed, before whom Haman has begun to fall, he will not be able to resist him but will surely fall before him. # GEM This is a remarkable testimony from pagans to the special providence of God over His people Israel. Even Haman's own household recognizes what Haman himself would not acknowledge: that there is a divine protection over the Jewish people which no human power can overcome. As Balaam was compelled to bless Israel against his will (Num. 23), so here the enemies of Israel confess their invincibility. The Fathers see in this a type of the Church, which no power of hell can overcome (Matt. 16:18).

ADHUC ILLIS LOQUENTIBUS VENERUNT EUNUCHI REGIS ET CITO EUM AD CONVIVIUM QUOD REGINA PARAVERAT PERGERE COMPULERUNT.] While they were still speaking, the king's eunuchs arrived and hurried Haman to the banquet which Queen Esther had prepared. Divine providence hastens the appointed hour of judgment: Haman's doom, foretold by his own counselors, is already at the door. The same messengers who once served his pride now summon him to his ruin. Thus God brings all things to their appointed end in His own time.

The king, inflamed by divine inspiration and instinct, would spend whole nights without sleep in exaggerating sacred matters, and when leisure permitted, he would compose prayers, and this he continued to do until extreme old age.

Ferrandus of Carthage, in his book to Reginus, commends this teaching in Rule 5: Let free access be open to priests making petitions to the duke; let prayer be frequented, the study of reading, although occupations flood in from every side, let it glow with the fire of spiritual desire, tepidity having been driven away. I have known a distinguished Prelate who spent the day on business and governance, and the night on prayer and studies.

# GEM "And he commanded that the histories and annals be brought to him." This praise is worthy of a king, that he should review and re-read his own acts, and if anything was omitted, especially in rewarding those who had deserved well, he should supply it. Thus Alexander Severus the Emperor, as Lampridius testifies, so that nothing should remain unrewarded, carefully committed to writing whatever gifts he had bestowed.

If anyone had asked nothing or very little from him, he would call that person, saying: "What is it that you ask for nothing? Do you wish to make me your debtor? Ask, lest you complain privately about me."

Moreover, that benefits conferred upon the kings of Persia were recorded and preserved in the careful memory of Annals and Diaries is attested by Herodotus, Book 4, treating of Darius (who is our Ahasuerus), the son of Hystaspes, making his review. And Thucydides, Book 1, narrates that Xerxes, son of Darius, said to Pausanias: "Your benefit will always be inscribed in our house."

Indeed the kings of Persia, as Xenophon teaches in Book 8 of the Cyropaedia, had certain ministers or officials who were called the ears and eyes of the kings. Apuleius, in his book On the World, describing the pomp of the Persian court from Aristotle, says: "But among them, certain men were called the royal ears and the imperial eyes; through the various kinds of offices, that King was believed by men to be a god, since he learned everything that was done anywhere through the report of the Otaseutae."

# GEM "For Haman had entered the outer court of the royal house." In Hebrew and Chaldean it is 'exterior'; for the interior could not be entered except by those summoned by the king. Therefore 'interior', that is, within the palace in the court which with respect to the innermost royal chamber was exterior, but with respect to the exterior was interior. For there were various courts in the Palace, some of which were more interior or exterior than others.

To suggest to the King that Mordecai be hanged on the gallows. Haman was hastening and had anticipated the dawn, so that he might approach the king first thing in the morning and request that Mordecai be hanged, before the King was occupied with other matters, that he might fulfill his supreme desire of avenging the contempt shown him, through the shameful death of the cross of Mordecai his contemner.

For pride equally with anger and indignation would not allow him to rest until he had taken this revenge. But God, the just judge and avenger of pride and injustice, turned all of Haman's vengeance back upon himself, and this gradually by degrees, until he hanged him on that very cross he had erected for Mordecai. In Haman therefore it is clear how true is that saying: "Guilt follows the guilty head." And: "Rarely has punishment on halting foot abandoned the wicked man who went before." For the Most High strikes down and thunders against the proud.

# GEM "The man whom the king wishes to honor ought to be clothed in royal garments, and set upon the horse which belongs to the king's stable" (upon which the king is accustomed to sit and ride, as the Hebrew and Septuagint have; for 'Sella' is as it were the seat of one sitting on a horse), "and receive the royal diadem upon his head."

The diadem was a linen band with which kings bound their heads. The diadem was a purple ornament distinguished with a white band and precious stones, says Curtius, Books 3 and 6. It is probable that together with the diadem the royal crown also was placed upon the head of Mordecai; for this was the proper insignia of the king, which Haman coveted and sought for himself, thinking that these things were being prepared by the king for no other than himself; and that Mordecai wore this by the king's command.

The Persians had rays like those of Mithras, that is, of the sun, whom they worshipped as God and king. For the kings among the Persians wished to be venerated with divine honors, as certain Mithras figures and suns. # GEM

Aman, ignorant of all these things, prepared against his will for his enemy Mardochaeus—whom he was demanding for the cross—and this tormented him greatly, namely that he himself had pronounced the sentence by which the king should bestow upon Mardochaeus the highest and royal honor, which through Aman himself, as though a dictator, he commanded to be conferred upon Mardochaeus. Thus David, creating Solomon his son as king and successor, said: "Take with you the servants of your lord, and set Solomon my son upon my mule," etc. And: "He shall come and sit upon my throne, and he shall reign in my place."

MAKE HASTE, AND TAKING THE ROBE AND THE HORSE, DO AS THOU HAST SPOKEN, TO MARDOCHAEUS THE JEW.] Robe, that is, the royal garment, as Aman had said in verse 8, which the Septuagint and the Chaldean call a byssine robe. Hence also Xenophon in Book 8 calls the tunic of King Cyrus a half-white purple: "But others," he says, "are not permitted to wear half-white." Understand that the king sent word to Aman that he should also give Mardochaeus a golden chain, a golden sword, and golden armlets. For it was the custom to adorn with these insignia one whom the king wished to honor, as is clear from 3 Esdras 9, and from Josephus Book 11 of the Antiquities, chapter 4, and from Aemilius Probus in his life of Artaxerxes.

WHO SITTETH BEFORE THE GATES.] There at that time sat Mardochaeus as though lowly and abject, but after this honor conferred upon him by the king, he was brought into the court of the palace and made familiar with the king.

He cried out (Aman): 'THIS HONOR IS WORTHY OF WHOMEVER THE KING WISHES TO HONOR.'] See here the sudden and complete reversal of fortune, by which Mordecai, destined for death, is raised to royal dignity and becomes as it were a second king — and this by his own enemy Aman, who therefore suffered here very many and very great pains and anguishes. For first, the honor he had hoped for himself tormented him, now taken away. Second, that it had been transferred to his capital enemy Mordecai. Third, that he himself was obliged to bestow this honor upon him. Fourth, that he who had previously been adored by all as a god was now the groom and herald of Mordecai, a lowly Jew. Fifth, that all these things fell upon him unexpectedly and all at once. The Rabbis fable that Aman's daughter poured a chamber-pot on her father's head as he passed by, thinking she was pouring it on Mordecai's head, and that therefore Aman said: 'And you too, my daughter, have put me to shame.'

MOURNING AND WITH HEAD COVERED] out of shame, grief and vehement anguish; for he was ashamed to show his face openly, he who had just acted as Mordecai's groom, when previously he had been venerated as a god.

IF MORDECAI, BEFORE WHOM YOU HAVE BEGUN TO FALL, IS OF THE SEED OF THE JEWS, YOU CANNOT RESIST HIM, BUT YOU SHALL FALL IN HIS SIGHT.] His friends said this either by some divine instinct, says Seraphinus, after the manner of the Sibyls and Caiaphas (John 11), or by a certain human wisdom and shrewd conjecture drawn from the fact that they had seen many Jews of stubborn and hard disposition who did not rest until they had destroyed their enemies, and from the common report they had heard that Pharaoh, the Canaanites, and other enemies of the Jews had been crushed by them through Moses, Joshua, David, etc. Lyranus adds that they said this because...
The king and Aman entered to drink with the queen.

The king said to Esther also on the second day, after he was heated with wine: What is your petition, Esther, that it may be granted to you? and what will you have done? even if you ask for half of my kingdom, you shall obtain it.

# GEM Darius was a great drinker of wine. Hence this epitaph was inscribed on his tomb: "I was able to drink much wine and bear it well," as Athenaeus testifies in book 10. Cyrus the Younger made the same boast about himself, as Plutarch records in his Life of Artaxerxes. Seneca in book 3 On Anger, chapter 14, writes that Praxaspes, one of the dearest friends of King Cambyses, who was overly addicted to wine, warned him to drink more sparingly, saying that drunkenness was shameful in a king whom the eyes and ears of all men followed. To this Cambyses replied: "That you may know how I never lose my wits, I will now prove that both my eyes and hands are steady after wine." He then drank more freely than usual, from larger cups; and now heavy and wine-sodden, he ordered the son of his critic to step forward beyond the threshold and stand with his left hand raised above his head. Then he drew his bow and pierced the very heart of the youth (for that, he said, was what he aimed at), and cutting open the breast, displayed the arrow lodged in the heart itself; and looking back at the father, he asked whether his hand was steady enough. The father replied that Apollo himself could not have shot more truly. May the gods utterly destroy him — in spirit and still more in his condition. Thus far Seneca.
She answered him: If I have found grace in your eyes, O king, and if it please you, give me my life for which I pray, and my people for whom I beseech you.

This was a sharp dart with which Esther struck Ahasuerus, and still more Aman, as if she said: This Aman by fraud obtained from you permission to kill all the Jews; therefore I also, being a Jewess, am to be killed by him, and thus you will be deprived of me as your queen, and of my companionship, which you so greatly sought and still seek. So it is not so much against me as against you, O Ahasuerus my king and husband, that Aman is injurious and violent, as much as he is cunning and deceitful.

For we are sold, I and my people, to be destroyed, to be slain, and to perish. And would to God we were sold for bondmen and bondwomen! the evil would be tolerable, and I would weep in silence; but now our enemy is one whose cruelty redounds upon the king.
Would that we were sold for bondmen and bondwomen — that is, that our lives being spared, we might at least be able to serve as servants and handmaids to the king and the Persians.

But now our enemy is one — who wishes to strip me and the Jews and all of life — "whose cruelty therefore redounds upon the king," both because by his edict for the death of the Jews, who enrich the royal treasury with vast tributes, who render many services to the king, from whom many strong soldiers can be enrolled for the defense of the king and kingdom, and who can even be set as wise men over his provinces as Daniel and his three companions were set — from all these and many other benefits which you, O Ahasuerus, receive from the Jews and might receive in greater measure, Aman deprives you. Likewise he brings upon you grave and lasting infamy among all nations for exterminating our innocent people, indeed a people that has served you well. Therefore the cruelty of Aman in seeking the extermination of the Jews redounds chiefly upon the king.
And Esther said: The most wicked Aman is our enemy and adversary. He hearing it, was forthwith astonished, not being able to bear the countenance of the king and queen.

Note here the magnanimity of Esther, by which she accuses Aman not secretly in his absence, as timid and base little women do, but openly in his presence before Ahasuerus, so that the king might know the accusation to be sincere and true, which Aman could not deny; wherefore Aman, struck as by lightning by this unexpected accusation, was stupefied and struck dumb. Let princes learn from Esther not to fear but to crush and trample down the power and insolence of the great. This is the mark of kings. Truly Seneca writes in Hercules Furens: "The first art of kingship is to be able to endure hatred." And in Oedipus: "He who fears hatred too much knows not how to reign." And in the Thebaid: "The creator of the world placed these two things together: hatred and kingship."

But the king being angry rose up, and went from the place of the banquet into the garden of trees. Aman also rose up to entreat Esther the queen for his life; for he understood that evil was prepared for him by the king.

And when the king came back out of the garden of trees and entered into the place of the banquet, he found Aman was fallen upon the bed on which Esther lay, and he said: He will force the queen also in my presence, in my own house. The word was not yet gone out of the king's mouth, and immediately they covered his face.

Aman had fallen upon the couch — not a nocturnal sleeping couch but a banqueting couch on which guests reclined at table — collapsed upon the one on which Esther lay, having prostrated himself at her knees to beg that his life be spared. Hence St. Athanasius in his Synopsis says: "When Aman saw the king grieved and moved to anger on account of the shameful act against the Jews, in the king's absence he supplicated the queen, falling at her knees. When the king entered and saw Aman touching the queen's knees, suspecting that he did it for shameful purposes, he ordered him to be hanged on the gallows." Severus Sulpicius records the same: "Then the king returned and saw Aman clasping the knees of the queen." For in ancient times suppliants held the knees of one to whom they supplicated, as if these were the seat of mercy, and this for four reasons which I have set out at length on Exodus 4:15.

The king said: "He will even force the queen in my presence." The king said this partly out of anger, partly out of jealousy, for he was desperately in love with Esther; for jealousy is suspicious of evil and of rivals, even where in reality there is nothing of the sort, as there was nothing of the sort in Aman's case. The king therefore suspects that Aman wishes to oppress Esther, that is, to do violence not so much to her life as to her chastity, attracted by her exceptional beauty, just as Ahasuerus himself felt attracted by the same.
And immediately they covered his face. This they did to one with whom the king was offended, as if such a one was unworthy to look upon the king's face or to be seen by him.

And Harbona, one of the eunuchs that stood in the king's presence, said: Behold the gibbet which he had prepared for Mardochai, who spoke for the king, standeth in Aman's house, being fifty cubits high. And the king said to him: Hang him upon it.
So Aman was hanged on the gibbet which he had prepared for Mardochai, and the king's wrath ceased.

The gibbet was transferred to a place of greater ignominy for him, so that he who a little before was adored by all as a god might now hang there as a spectacle and mockery for all who entered and left the city.

Cornelius à Lapide notes that those who had disregarded the royal commands (namely, concerning the building of the Temple) were to be impaled, and their possessions confiscated to the royal treasury. The same edict was afterward issued by Darius, in 1 Esdras.
And Mordecai went in before the face of the KING — that is to say, he became most intimate, most closely joined, and most familiar to the king, as one next to the king, even as Aman had been.

AND THE KING TOOK THE RING WHICH HE HAD COMMANDED TO BE TAKEN BACK FROM AMAN, AND GAVE IT TO MORDECAI. This was the royal ring with which Aman had sealed his letters concerning the slaying of the Jews, as with the royal seal. This ring, therefore, was of the highest dignity and authority, and made Mordecai the king's viceroy and, as it were, a second king. Whence Alexander ab Alexandro, lib. 2, Genial. c. 9, says: "Among the Persians, to be given a ring by the king was a sign of friendship, signifying that he admitted them into his closest fellowship and the bond of the greatest covenant."

Hence also Mordecai was clothed with royal garments and a crown — [text continues] — with the decree that every man who should alter this command should have a beam taken from his house and erected and fastened through him, and his house should be confiscated.

Esther appointed Mardochai over her house, that he might be its steward — indeed its ruler, as he had been before she became queen. Serarius thinks that Esther gave Mardochai the house of Aman, which had been given to her by the king, and this...
The king wiped away all blame and infamy of the former edict from himself, and transferred it onto Haman, when he issued a contrary decree in favor of the Jews.

ACCITISQUE SCRIBIS ET LIBRARIIS. The scribes were the chief secretaries who dictated letters; the librarii were those who copied down what was dictated by the scribes, both so that copies might be sent to the provinces, and so that they might be preserved in the archives and in the public records and annals.

QUIBUS (VEREDARIIS SIVE CURSORIBUS) IMPERAVIT REX, UT CONVENIRENT JUDAEOS: that is, so that they might make known to them the royal edict issued in their favor, lest the magistrates, who were hostile to the Jews, might under some pretext conceal, delay, or suppress it; but that they should be compelled by the Jews themselves to execute it, who would threaten to accuse them before the king as contemners of the royal edict if they did not do so. The Septuagint and our text add here, in chapter 16, that the king by this edict granted the Jews the right to live by their own laws everywhere.
MARDOCHAEUS AUTEM, etc., FULGEBAT VESTIBUS REGIIS, HYACINTHINIS VIDELICET ET AERIIS: that is, white, as the Hebrew and Chaldean texts have it. See below.

Mordecai went forth from the presence of the king wearing royal apparel of blue and white, with a great crown of gold. ON THE CROWN OF GOLD UPON HIS HEAD: in the Persian manner, radiated, that is, emitting rays like a miter or like the sun (which the Persians worshipped as a god). Behold Mordecai as a second king, who but a short time before went about squalid and pallid, and destined for the cross. To such great dignity, says St. Jerome on Daniel chapter 1 toward the end: God willed that Mordecai be exalted, so that just as formerly in Joseph, and recently in Daniel, so now in Mordecai the captive and exiled Jews might have consolation, seeing a man of their own people now being prince of the Egyptians, now of the Chaldeans, now of the Persians. # GEM Hence the Chaldean Targum on Canticles 7:5 says: The poor of the people, who walked beside the Prince here and there, because they were needy, it shall come to pass that they be clothed in purple, just as Daniel was clothed in the city of Babylon, and Mordecai in Susa, on account of the merit of Abraham, whom He established as king from the beginning of the age; and on account of the righteousness of Isaac, whom his father bound to offer him; and on account of the kindness of Jacob, who peeled the rods in the channels.

TO THE JEWS THERE AROSE LIGHT AND JOY (to those worn out with shame, grief, and anguish, and as it were half-dead). GLADNESS, HONOR AND FEASTING: For rejoicing they exulted, dancing and leaping in chorus.

Allegorically: Esther is the Blessed Virgin, of whom in the mystical sense it is said in Sirach 50:6: As the morning star in the midst of a cloud; for like a shining star she came forth dispelling darkness, of whom St. Bernard in his second homily on the Gospel says: She is the noble star of Jacob, whose ray illumines the whole world, whose splendor shines forth in the heavens above and penetrates even into hell, and traversing also the lands, warming minds more than bodies, fosters virtues and burns away vices. # GEM

Finally, Serarius notes in this tragedy of Haman a sevenfold catastrophe: 1. Haman had been exalted above all the Princes: afterwards he was cast down below all. 2. He had a throne second only to the king's: afterwards he was obliged to spread the horse's saddle for Mordecai, serving as his groom and running before him. 3. He had hurled very many threatening words against him: and was obliged to praise him in the manner of a herald. 4. He was called father by the king, and afterwards by the same king was named guilty of lese-majesty, a rebel, a traitor. 5. He looked upon Mordecai with the most hostile and fierce eyes in indignation: and afterwards could not bear the king's countenance. 6. Not content with the death of Mordecai alone, he wished to drink the blood of all the Jews; and he himself, not alone but with his whole house, with his ten beloved sons, was brought to the cross; and many thousands of his same spirit among the gentiles were slaughtered. 7. The very gallows and the very day he had appointed for the death of Mordecai and the other Jews — on these he himself, with his own, perished most miserably.

IN SO MUCH THAT MANY OF ANOTHER PEOPLE AND SECT JOINED THEMSELVES TO THEIR (the Jews') RELIGION AND CEREMONIES: that is, they were circumcised and converted to Judaism and to the true worship of God. For they saw that this miraculous and unexpected salvation and exaltation of the Jews could not have come about without the special providence of God, who cares for the affairs of His people.

Therefore on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, which we have already said is called Adar, when slaughter was being prepared against all the Jews, and their enemies were thirsting for their blood, the situation was reversed, and the Jews began to have the upper hand and to avenge themselves upon their adversaries.

And they gathered together in the individual cities, towns, and places, to stretch out their hand against their enemies and persecutors. And no one dared to resist, because the fear of their greatness had penetrated all peoples.
For even the judges of the provinces, and the leaders, and the procurators, and every dignitary who presided over individual places and works, extolled the Jews out of fear of Mordecai;
whom they knew to be the chief of the palace and of very great power; and the fame of his name grew daily and flew through the mouths of all.
And so the Jews struck their enemies with a great blow and killed them, repaying them what they had prepared to do to them;
so much so that even in Susa they killed five hundred men, besides the ten sons of Haman the Agagite, enemy of the Jews, whose names are these:
Pharsandalha, and Delphon, and Esphaltha,
and Phoratha, and Adalia, and Aridatha,
and Phermesta, and Arisai, and Aridai, and Jezatha.
When they had killed these, they were unwilling to touch the plunder from their possessions.
And immediately the number of those who had been killed in Susa was reported to the king.

Who said to the queen: In the city of Susa the Jews have killed five hundred men, and ten other sons of Haman; what slaughter do you think they have carried out in all the provinces? What further do you ask, and what do you wish me to command to be done?
To whom she replied: If it please the king, let power be given to the Jews, that as they have done today in Susa, so also tomorrow they may do, and let the ten sons of Haman be hanged on gibbets.
And the king commanded that it should be so done. Immediately the edict was proclaimed in Susa, and the ten sons of Haman were hanged.
And the Jews gathering on the fourteenth day of the month Adar, three hundred men were killed in Susa; but they did not plunder their substance.

But also throughout all the provinces which were subject to the king's dominion, the Jews stood for their lives, their enemies and persecutors being slain; insomuch that seventy-five thousand of the slain were numbered, and no one touched anything of their substance.

Now the thirteenth day of the month Adar was the first day of the killing among all, and on the fourteenth day they ceased to slay. They appointed that day to be solemn, so that ever after they would give themselves to feasting, joy, and banqueting.

But those who had carried out the slaughter in the city of Susa were occupied in slaughter on the thirteenth and fourteenth day of the same month; but on the fifteenth day they ceased to smite. And therefore they appointed that same day to be solemn for feasting and rejoicing.

# GEM GRANDIS ENIM CUNCTOS JUDAICI NOMINIS TERROR INVASERAT: That they should not do to themselves what they had done to Haman, and what they were about to do shortly to their other enemies by the king's edict, v. 10. Therefore, lest they be counted as enemies of the Jews, and thus be killed, some truly, others falsely showed themselves to be friends of the Jews, and became proselytes.

[On verse 1: "Therefore on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, which we have already said is called Adar, when the slaughter of all Jews was being prepared, and their enemies were thirsting for their blood, the Jews by a reverse of fortune began to have the upper hand."] The twelfth and last month of the year, Adar, corresponds to our February. For the first month of the Hebrews was Nisan, that is March, because in it they celebrated Passover, Exodus 12. Moreover, the Jews used lunar months, not solar ones; wherefore the new moon, or new lunar phase, was the first day of the month; the thirteenth day was the day immediately preceding the full moon, for this fell on the fourteenth day. Therefore this day, the eve of the full moon of February, had been appointed for the slaughter of the Jews, and then by a reverse of fortune for the slaughter of the enemies of the Jews. Wherefore even now the Jews on that day in their synagogues read the history of Esther annually; and whenever mention is made of Haman, they strike the benches with fists and mallets, just as if they were striking Haman's head, as Antonio Margarita testifies in his book on the rites of the Jews, and Gregory of Toulouse in Syntagma book 2, chapter 16, number 10. Moreover Judas Maccabaeus instituted another feast on this same thirteenth day of Adar, on account of the remarkable victory obtained on the same day over Nicanor, concerning which see 2 Maccabees, last chapter, verse 37.

[On verse 6: "So that in Susa they killed five hundred men besides the ten sons of Haman,"] whom they killed first publicly in the theatre, as it seems, as defendants condemned by the king; for they began the slaughter with the sons of Haman their sworn enemy, as with the first-fruits and first victims of their just vengeance; then afterwards they hanged the bodies of those already slain on gibbets the next day, as is said in verse 14, for the greater ignominy of Haman and terror of his enemies. Therefore in the third month the law had brought forth the edict that the Jews should slay the sons of Haman and the rest of their adversaries, as was stated.

[On verse 19: "But the Jews who dwelt in towns and villages and country places decreed the fourteenth day of the month Adar for feasting and joy, so that they would rejoice therein and send to one another portions of feasts and foods."] —
[On verse 20: "So Mordecai wrote all these things, and sent letters comprehending them to the Jews who dwelt in all the king's provinces, both near and far,"] —
[On verse 21: "that they should receive the fourteenth and fifteenth day of the month Adar for feasts, and should celebrate them with solemn honour every year as they came round;"] —

[On verse 22: "because on those days the Jews revenged themselves on their enemies, and their mourning and sorrow were turned into mirth and joy, and that these should be days of feasting and gladness, and that they should send portions of food to one another and give gifts to the poor."] —
[On verse 23: "And the Jews undertook all things in solemn rite which they had then begun to do, and which Mordecai had commanded by letter to be done."] —

[On verse 24: "For Haman the son of Amadathi of the stock of Agag, the enemy and adversary of the Jews, had devised evil against them to kill them and destroy them, and had cast phur, which in our language is interpreted as a lot."] —

[On verse 25: "And afterwards Esther went in to the king, beseeching him that his attempts might be made void by the king's letters; and the evil which he had devised against the Jews might return upon his own head. And at last both he and his sons were hanged on the cross."] —

[On verse 26: "And from that time these days are called Phurim, that is of lots; because phur, that is a lot, was cast into the urn. And all things that were done are contained in the epistle, that is, in the volume of this book;"] —

[On verse 27: "and the things they suffered, and which were afterwards changed, the Jews received upon themselves and their seed and upon all who joined their religion, that none should be allowed to pass these two days without solemnity; which scripture testifies and fixed times require, succeeding one another continually through the years."] —

[On verse 28: "These are the days which no forgetfulness shall ever abolish; and through all generations every province in the whole world shall celebrate them; neither is there any city in which the days of Phurim, that is of lots, are not observed by the Jews and their posterity, which is bound by these ceremonies."] —
[On verse 29: "And Esther the queen daughter of Abihail, and Mordecai the Jew, wrote also a second letter, that with all diligence this solemn day should be established for the future;"] —
[On verse 30: "and they sent to all the Jews who dwelt in the hundred and twenty-seven provinces of king Ahasuerus, that they should have peace and receive truth,"] —

[On verse 31: "observing the days of lots and celebrating them with joy in their proper time; as Mordecai and Esther had ordained, and they undertook to observe for themselves and their posterity, fasts and cries, and the days of lots,"] —
[On verse 32: "and all things which are contained in the history of this book which is called Esther."] —

The Jews who had been shut up in prison, lest they escape by flight, were imprisoned, and after nine months, namely on the 13th day of the twelfth month which had been appointed for the slaughter, they were slain, and on the following day after being slain they were hanged.

Aman and all his kindred [cognationem] were hanged before the gate of Susa on gallows; therefore his sons were killed with him in the third month, not the twelfth. Response: by "kindred" [cognationem] there are not meant his sons (for otherwise he would have named them explicitly), but the relatives and connections [cognatos et affines] of Aman; for these, as his supporters, were hanged with him; but his sons were reserved for the general slaughter of all, and for the public spectacle of just vengeance on the 13th of Adar.

WHOM WHEN THEY HAD SLAIN, THEY WOULD NOT TOUCH THE SPOILS OF THEIR GOODS — both so that they might show that they sought nothing other than the preservation of their own lives; for those who were being slain wished to destroy this; wherefore they did not covet riches, nor on account of riches would they put to death some innocent wealthy man (as often happens) among the guilty; # GEM and finally, because Aman, as the declared enemy of the Jews, was as it were anathema [quasi anathema], so that no one dared to touch his goods and those of his sons. The same they did with the rest of the slain, whose possessions they refused to plunder, as is said in v. 16, even though the king had granted them this.
[On the soldiers who plunder not only enemies but also friends, indeed slaughter them in hope of plunder]
column4: Soldiers imitate those who not only plunder enemies, but often friends also, indeed slay them in hope of plunder.

column3: That seventy-five thousand of those who were slain might be fulfilled.
column4: The mourning of the enemies was great, and therefore also the number of the slain was great, because the Jews could not be secure in their lives unless their enemies were killed. And this is what is said here: *For their lives* (steadfastly slaying their enemies in defense of their own lives) *the Jews stood among the slain of their enemies*, etc. For unless those enemies had themselves been killed, there was fear that they might again attack the Jews, or even rebel against the king. Furthermore Mordecai commanded the Jews to inquire diligently and to note who were truly enemies of the Jews, lest any friend or innocent person be killed among the enemies and the guilty, as happened in the Sicilian Vespers and the Parisian Vespers; and therefore he commanded the slaughter to be deferred for nine months, namely from the third month to the twelfth.

column3: And from that time those days (the 13th and 14th of Adar, on which the Jews who were to be slaughtered slaughtered their enemies) were called Purim, that is, of lots: because Pur (that is, a lot) was cast into the urn.
column4: Thus this feast, says Sanchez, is called Purim, that is, of lots, not from the liberty which the Jews obtained on that day by killing or subduing their enemies; but from the lots which Haman consulted, and which appointed the slaughter of the Jews for that same thirteenth day of the month Adar; so that, when that day returns in its annual recurrence, the memory may be renewed of the danger in which they stood, and what consternation and anguish of soul they had when they received the news of the lots and the day. # GEM In the Roman Septuagint, wrongly, instead of *phurim* as the Complutensian Septuagint reads, one finds *phrurim*; whence Josephus also calls these days *phrourai*. Unless one prefers to say that the Septuagint and Josephus changed the word *phurim* by adding the letter *r* into *phrourai*, so as to allude to the Greek etymology of the verb *phroureō*, which means to guard and to watch, as if to say: These days were called by the enemies Phurim, that is, of lots, also of breakings, by which they would break our race (for this is what the Hebrew and Arabic *phur* signifies); but by God through Esther and Mordecai they were made from *phurim* into *phrourai*, that is, guardians and preservers, by which it pleased God that we should be preserved safe in life, while for the enemies those same days became *phurim*, that is, of breakings, by which they themselves were broken and slaughtered by us. # GEM Thus allegorically, the days of Christ's Passion were days of Purim, that is, of lots and of breakings: for the Jews cast lots concerning the killing of Christ in preference to Barabbas, and the soldiers cast lots for the division of Christ's crucified tunic; and then they broke the flesh of Christ with nails, scourges, and thorns. But these days of Purim were made by Christ into *phrurim* or *phrourai*, that is, guardians and preservers; because they freed all who believe in Christ, hope in Him, and love Him from sin, death, and hell. Those same days became Purim for the Jews, because upon those very occasions, namely at the Passover thereafter—

column3: The lots (purim) cast by Haman were converted: for Jerusalem was captured, laid waste, and utterly destroyed by Titus 68 years after Christ.
The same was pre-signified by the lot customarily cast on the day of expiation over the two goats, one the scapegoat, the other to be sacrificed for sin, Lev. 16:8-9. See what is said there.

Tropologically, the saints celebrate the feast of Purim when adverse lots are changed into prosperous ones, as they were changed for holy Job (last chapter), for Joseph (Gen. 45), for Moses (Exod. 4-5), for David, for Tobias, and other saints, and notably for St. Eustace, as is clear from his life in Surius, on 20 September. # GEM

Anagogically, the feast of Purim, that is of lots, will be the day of judgment, on which Christ will change the lots of the pious afflicted in this life, and of the impious exulting in this life, and will assign to the pious perpetual happiness, to the impious everlasting gehenna, as the Wise Man teaches (Wisd. 5:1), and Isaiah (65:12), and Christ himself (Luke 6:21, 25). # GEM

AND ALL THINGS THAT WERE DONE, ARE CONTAINED IN THE VOLUME OF THIS EPISTLE, THAT IS OF THIS BOOK. From this it is gathered that Mordecai is the author of this book. For he himself was the author of this epistle; and the epistle contained the same things as this book contains; but what Mordecai had first written briefly in the epistle, setting the same things down in this book he wrote more fully and worked out more carefully.

AND WHAT THEY HAD SUFFERED (the Jews from Haman and his followers), AND WHAT WAS AFTERWARDS CHANGED, the lot of the slaughter of the Jews being turned upon their enemies; repeat: these things are contained in the volume of this book. And in memory of these things, and in thanksgiving, the Jews took upon themselves AND UPON THEIR SEED; that is, they bound themselves and their children and posterity, and also proselytes, WHO WISHED TO JOIN THEIR RELIGION, to celebrate this feast of Purim, that is of lots, so that IT SHOULD NOT BE LAWFUL FOR ANY TO PASS THESE TWO DAYS WITHOUT SOLEMNITY.

Not that all are bound to keep both days as feast days, but that one of the two, or certainly the third after the slaughter: for the Jews dwelling in Susa, because they had struck down their enemies both on the 13th and the 14th, therefore on the 15th of Adar, when they had ceased from slaughter, they celebrated the feast of Purim; but the others who had abstained from slaughter one day earlier, namely on the fourteenth, kept the feast on that same day.
WHICH THE SCRIPTURE (that is, the epistle of Mordecai, afterwards confirmed by the whole Synagogue) TESTIFIES; that is, attests and commands to be recalled with annual remembrance and gratitude.

AND THEY SEEK CERTAIN TIMES IN THE YEARS CONTINUALLY SUCCEEDING THEM; namely that they should be celebrated every year at a fixed and appointed time, to wit on the said 14th and 15th days, and this not only by decree of Mordecai, but also of Ahasuerus, who ordained the same not only for Jews but also for Persians and the rest.

Anagog. The feast of lots signifies the feast and joy of the blessed in heaven over the defeated devil, the world, tyrants, and other persecutors, who had cast lots for their destruction; but through one man and one woman they were liberated, that is, through Christ and Mary, and they shall punish their enemies with swords, as it is written in Psalm 149:6: "And two-edged swords in their hands, to execute vengeance upon the nations, chastisements among the peoples, to bind their kings with fetters and their nobles with chains of iron, to execute upon them the judgment written: this glory is for all his saints." This shall come to pass at the end of the age, that is, on the day of judgment; therefore it is celebrated in the twelfth month. It is kept on the fourteenth day, that is, at the full moon, because when the Saints shall judge the nations with Christ and shall rule over the peoples, the Church of the Saints shall then have the fullness of her beauty.

To the solemnity is added the fifteenth day, to signify the joy on this second day which the Saints shall receive in seeing forever the damnation of the impious persecutors, as it is written in Isaiah: "And they shall go forth and see the carcasses of the men who have transgressed against me. Their worm shall not die, and their fire shall not be quenched; and they shall be a spectacle to all flesh." So Ribera, book 5, On the Temple, last chapter.

That they might have peace and receive truth. Hebrew: They sent letters of words of peace and truth, that is, wishing them peace, meaning all manner of prosperity, and exhorting them to truth, that is, fidelity and constancy in keeping the promises regarding celebrating the feast of Purim on the appointed days.
And they undertook to observe from themselves and their seed fastings and cries. For the Jews were accustomed to fast on the eve of feasts, as indeed they do.

King Ahasuerus made all the land and all the islands of the sea tributary. This shows the vast extent of his empire, which stretched from India to Ethiopia over 127 provinces, as was said in chapter 1.

His strength and his rule, and the dignity and sublimity by which he exalted Mordecai, are written in the books of the Medes and Persians. Jerome notes in his preface that what follows from verse 4 onward is found not in the Hebrew but only in the Greek edition, and he marked it with an obelus as of uncertain authority, though it is received in the Vulgate.

And how Mordecai of Jewish descent was second to king Ahasuerus, and great among the Jews, and acceptable to the multitude of his brethren, seeking the good of his people and speaking those things which pertained to the peace of his seed. Mordecai is here praised with a fourfold commendation: his rank, his reputation among his people, his benevolence, and his pursuit of peace — all of which make him a type of Christ.

And Mordecai said: These things have been done by God. # GEM He rightly ascribes all to God, for it is God who raises up and casts down, who humbles the proud and exalts the humble, as Hannah sang and as the whole history of Esther demonstrates.

I remembered the dream that I had seen, signifying these same things, and nothing of it was left unfulfilled. The dream was recounted at the beginning of the book in the Greek additions. Mordecai now shows that divine providence had foreordained all these events.

The little fountain that grew into a river, and was turned into light and the sun, and overflowed into many waters: this is Esther, whom the king took as wife and willed to be queen. The fountain signifies Esther's humble and obscure origin; the river and sun signify the splendor and influence of her royal dignity, by which she became the instrument of her people's salvation.

And the two dragons: I am one, and Aman is the other. The two dragons represent Mordecai and Haman contending against each other — Mordecai as defender of Israel, Haman as its would-be destroyer. Some see in this a figure of Christ and Satan in their great conflict. # GEM

And the nations that were gathered together: these are they who attempted to destroy the name of the Jews. The gathering of the Gentile nations against Israel prefigures the gathering of all the powers of the world against Christ and His Church, according to Psalm 2.

But my people: this is Israel, who cried to the Lord, and the Lord saved his people; and he delivered us from all evils, and wrought great signs and wonders among the nations. The salvation of Israel is attributed entirely to God, who heard their cry and intervened. This mirrors the Exodus and all subsequent deliverances of the chosen people.

And he commanded that there should be two lots, one of the people of God, and the other of all the nations. The two lots signify the two peoples — Israel and the Gentiles — and their divergent fates under divine judgment. The lot of Purim thus receives a theological interpretation as an expression of divine predestination.

And both lots came to their appointed hour and day and time before God, that he might remember his people Israel and deliver them. The fulfillment of both lots on the appointed day demonstrates that all things are ordered by divine providence, which permits no chance but governs all by eternal wisdom.

[On the Christians' use of noise and clappers in the Triduum] It is noted by Serarius and others that the Jewish custom of making loud clamor and striking benches on the feast of Purim, in memory of Haman's destruction, may have influenced the Christian practice of using rattles and clappers during the three days of Christ's Passion, though this remains a conjecture. # GEM

[On Athanasius and the burning of Haman's effigy] St. Athanasius in his Synopsis records that on the 13th of Adar the Jews publicly carried a wooden cross in memory and mockery of Haman crucified, and burned it together with an image of Haman. Because many Jews used this rite to represent and mock the Passion of Christ, as though Christ were personated under the mask of Haman, the Emperors Honorius and Theodosius forbade these customs, as is clear from the Code concerning Jews and Coelicolae, which states that provincial governors are to prohibit Jews from burning a shape resembling the holy cross in contempt of the Christian faith. # GEM

Mordecai was second to King Ahasuerus, of Jewish birth — he was therefore as it were the king's viceroy, and in his absence acted as king; indeed even with the king present, Mordecai by his rare prudence and vigilance governed the empire, as Josephus attests.

Speaking of the welfare of his seed (that is, of the interests and prosperity of the Jews) — meaning that Mordecai devoted himself not to his own advantage but to that of his people. Scripture here passes over his illustrious deeds, and sends those who wish to know them to the annals of the Medes and Persians.

Benjamin in his Itinerary reports that Mordecai and Esther were buried in the chief city of the Medes, called Hamadan the Great, and says that in his time there were 50,000 Jews in it. Some conjecture that the province called Mardochaea, situated between Babylonia and the Persian Gulf, was named after Mordecai.

Here in the Hebrew the book of Esther ends; the remaining chapters that follow were translated from Greek into Latin by St. Jerome. Note here that these last seven chapters of Esther are canonical Scripture — this is clear both from the definition of the Council of Trent, Session 4, and from the Latin Vulgate edition and the Greek Septuagint, both of which consistently contain them, though in the Greek they are not placed separately as in the Latin, but each is inserted in its proper place in historical order, as also before St. Jerome they existed inserted in their places in the Old Latin edition.

But St. Jerome, because he translated Esther from the Hebrew into Latin, and in his time these seven chapters did not exist in Hebrew, therefore translated them from the Greek, placed them separately, and referred them to the end of the book after the sequence of the whole history. That they once existed in Hebrew before St. Jerome is clear from the Greek Septuagint and the ancient Latin Vulgate, and from Josephus and Josippus son of Gorion, who contain the same material as our text — drawn assuredly from no other source than the ancient Hebrew original.

It is probable that this book, like that of Tobit, was composed twice in Hebrew: once more briefly, as it now exists in Hebrew; a second time more fully — and from this fuller version flowed the Greek translation of the Septuagint, and from that our own Latin, which is why that version attaches these additional portions. See Feardentius here, and Bellarmine, book 1, On the Word of God.
And Mardochaeus said: ] when, with Aman now hanged, he understood the meaning of his dream.

I remembered the dream which I had seen, signifying these same things. ] This dream therefore was sent by God to Mardochaeus, so that through it He might signify the future exaltation of himself and Esther, and the struggle with Aman. Wherefore this dream is to be placed in historical order at the beginning of this book and of the whole history, as it is placed there in the Greek Romans. A similar dream was sent by God to Pharaoh, Genesis 41, and to Nabuchodonosor, Daniel 2.

A small spring, which grew into a river, and was turned into light and sun, and overflowed into many waters, is Esther, whom the king took as wife, and wished to be queen. ] Mardochaeus had therefore dreamed, as is narrated in the following chapter at verse 5, that he saw a small spring which grew into a river and was turned into light and sun; and afterwards he understood this spring to be Esther, who before was lowly and of no account, but afterwards grew so greatly that she became queen, and wonderfully increased the people of the Jews both in number and in strength and in glory, and therefore shone forth like the sun in Persia and throughout the whole world with the rays of her virtue and majesty. Esther is aptly compared to a spring: first, because just as a spring continually gushing forth waters produces a great river, so Esther by her works and zeal produced a great people of the Jews, and converted many of the Gentiles to God and to Judaism, as has been said.

Esther, like springs of water, extinguished the immense fire of Haman kindled against the Jews, and appeased both herself and the wrath of God; thirdly, she overwhelmed and drowned with those same waters, as it were, torrential and violent, 75,000 Gentiles who were hostile to the Jews;

in summo moerore et angore, that is, in the deepest grief and anguish, IN LUCEM SOLEMQUE, that is, into the bright sun (this is a hendiadys), it was turned, namely into the highest joy and felicity, when he freed the Jews from death and caused them to triumph over their enemies. Moreover, it is fittingly said that this fountain was turned into light and sun, because there is a great affinity and analogy between a fountain and the sun: first, because just as a fountain is the origin and source of a river, so the sun is the source of light; for just as a river flows from a fountain, so light flows from the sun; for the production, outflow, and emanation of each is similar; whence the Hebrew נֵר ner, that is, light and lamp, alludes to נָהָר nahar, that is, river, and to אוֹר or, that is, light, because it signifies the outflow or propagation of light as of a river, that is, of seed and posterity; second, because what a fountain is on earth, the sun is in heaven; for just as a fountain is as it were the eye of the earth, so the sun is as it were the eye of heaven, illuminating and adorning the entire heavens and world. Whence the Hebrew עַיִן ain signifies both eye and fountain, because what the eye is in man, the fountain is in the earth, and the sun in heaven; third, because a river flowing from a fountain is clear, transparent, and bright like crystal (indeed crystal is nothing other than fountain water congealed), and illuminated by the rays of the sun it shines like the sun; fourth, because from a fountain and from the primordial waters the sun and the heavens were created. Whence also the Hebrew שָׁמַיִם Scamaim, as if to say 'there are the waters,' are so called, as I have shown at greater length at Gen. 1:7. In a similar manner and by analogy, Esther here, as it were watery, that is, poor and lowly, was raised by Ahasuerus to the royal throne as if to heaven, and became glorious and radiant as the sun.

# GEM The Scripture often combines light and water together, as in Psalm 35:10: "Lord, with thee is the fountain of life, and in thy light we shall see light." And in Apocalypse 22:1, the light of glory of the blessed is described through the river of paradise, where it says: "And he showed me a river of the water of life, bright as crystal, proceeding from the throne of God and of the Lamb." Moreover, in fountains the sun continually expresses its own image through the reflection of rays, and makes a parhelion, that is, as it were a second sun, so that the fountain itself seems to be a sun, as I shall say in the following chapter, verses 10 and 11.

This therefore is the paradox and hieroglyphic of Mordecai's dream: out of waters, a sun — that is, out of tribulation rises joy, out of adversity prosperity, out of desolation consolation, out of grief gladness, out of humiliation exultation, out of mortification resurrection. The Poets imagine that the sun, when it sets panting from the labor of the day, plunges itself into the waters and the sea, so that it may bathe and cool itself by night; and therefore in the morning it rises again from those same waters fresh and reborn. So the waters of humiliation refresh the mind burning with the works of charity, so that it rises again and returns to those same works more humble and vigorous. Then that is accomplished which is said in Job 11:16: "Thou shalt also forget thy misery, and remember it as waters that have passed away. And thine age shall rise up clearer than the noonday; and though thou be in darkness, thou shalt be as the morning star."

And that of Malachias 4:2: "But unto you that fear my name, the Sun of justice shall arise, and health in his wings; and you shall go forth, and shall leap like calves of the herd." See what is said there. Finally, this dream alluded to the custom of the Persians, who worshipped the sun as a divine power, and called their kings suns. Hence Cyrus in Persian means the same as sun. And Sapor, king of the Persians, as Marcellinus testifies in book 17, writing to the Emperor Constantius, arrogated to himself this title: "King of kings Sapor, companion of the stars, and brother of the sun and moon, to Constantius my brother, greeting."

When therefore Mordecai saw in the vision Esther as it were as a sun, he understood shortly afterward that she would be queen of the Persians, and would bear upon her head a solar crown, that is, one adorned with golden rays like those of the sun. Josephus adds this as well.

Esther in the persecution of Haman was afflicted but consoled by the recollection of this dream of hers, and thence with certain hope of the liberation that was to follow. The same may be said of the entire four-year period during which Esther lay hidden as it were, lowly, unknown, and consigned to oblivion in the house of Ahasuerus; for she was brought into his house in the second year of Ahasuerus, but only in the seventh year at last

This is indeed the way and method of divine providence: that those whom God wishes to exalt and glorify, He first humbles, makes vile and lowly. Therefore, whoever experiences this very thing in himself, let him raise his spirit with certain hope of better things, and know that he is on the right path by which God leads His own to loftiness and glory; because as the Wise Man says: Humility precedes glory. Thus: the Lord first mortifies, and then quickens; He leads down to hell, and then brings back. 1 Kings 2:6

ALLEGORICAL: This spring turned toward the sun, and likened to the spring of the sun, which Joshua mentions literally signifying Esther, mystically represents the Virgin, who in Apoc. 12:1 is called the woman clothed with the sun. Hence St. Fulgentius, sermon on the praise of the Virgin, says: Mary was made the window of heaven, because through her God poured forth the true light of the world. Blessed Damascene, Oration 1 on the Nativity of the Virgin, calls her the Gate of Life, the Fountain of Light, by which name many others also call her. # GEM

Epiphanius, sermon on the praise of the Virgin, in these words: O holy Virgin, mother of eternal light; light, I say, which in the heavens illuminates the hosts of Angels; light, which illuminates the incomprehensible eye of the Seraphim themselves; light, which illuminates the sun with its splendid torches; light, which illuminates the ends of the earth to believe in the Trinity; light, which said: I am the light of the world; light, which said: I came as light into the world; light, which was assumed and illuminated all things that are in heaven and on earth. And again: O womb bearing the inextinguishable light of grace shining sevenfold. # GEM

Chrysippus, in his Oration to the Mother of God: Hail, fountain of light illuminating every man; hail, rising of the sun which can bear no setting. Ildefonsus, sermon 1 on the Assumption: Mary, he says, according to the Hebrew word is interpreted star of the sea; the present age is the sea, but the star is the Blessed Virgin Mary, from whom arose He by whom the whole world is illuminated. Hence draw nearer with confidence to the praise of the Virgin, and be illuminated, for she it is through whom the true light shone in the sea of this age. # GEM

The Church in the Mass, in the preface of the Blessed Virgin, uses this same metaphor in these words: Who while the glory of virginity remained, poured forth upon the world the eternal light, Jesus Christ our Lord. Ephrem, in his sermon on the praises of the Virgin: Hail, he says, most radiant star, from whom Christ proceeded; hail, through whom the most brilliant sun of justice shone upon us. And again he calls the Virgin the Light-bearer; as does Cyril, book 1 on right faith to the Queens: Mary, he says, mother of life, mother of beauty, great bearer of the Light-bringer. # GEM

TWO DRAGONS, I AND HAMAN: These two dragons fought among themselves, as is said in the following chapter v.6, to signify the struggle of Mordecai with Haman, and his overthrow. For dragons are insignia and heraldic emblems of soldiers and commanders: they denote their qualities, of which the chief are three, namely vigilance, fortitude, and swiftness in accomplishing things. For the dragon is watchful and sees most keenly; hence Dragon is named from the Greek δράκειν, that is, from seeing. Likewise it is most strong and most swift; hence in Arabia it flies, wherefore it presents something divine; for which reason the ancients assigned to the dragon the guardianship of houses, temples, oracles, treasures, and the infancy of heroes; as the poets have fabled of Hercules, indeed of Nero.

Hence again the dragon was among the ancients a symbol of prudence, wisdom, and even of prophecy, as Carolus Paschal teaches, book 9 on Crowns, ch. 15. Add (what is fitting and proper here) that the Parthians, or Persians, formerly sent lions and dragons against enemies in battle to destroy them, as Brissonius teaches from Lucian and Ennius, book 2 on the Persian kingdom, p.310. Olympias, mother of Alexander the Great, had a similar dream about a dragon, by which empire and monarchy was portended to her, just as the same dragon portended it to Augustus, Aurelian, Severus, Scipio and others, as Pierius testifies, Hieroglyphics 15.

Hence also Pharaoh is called the great dragon, Ezek. 29:3. Hence too in war those were formerly called Draconarii, who are now called standard-bearers. Finally, just as the dragons of Moses devoured the dragons of the Magicians, Exodus 7:12, so Mordecai devoured Haman. Thus even now soldiers of great valor, namely cavalry, who take infantry at the rear so that dismounting them from their horses they fight as both cavalry and infantry against the enemy, are commonly called Dragons.

AND HE ORDAINED TWO LOTS: ONE FOR THE PEOPLE OF GOD, AND THE OTHER FOR ALL THE NATIONS: For the people of God, that is, of those opposing and hostile to the Jews; for Haman cast a lot for the killing of the Jews; but Mordecai cast a lot for the slaughter of the Gentiles who were enemies of the Jews.

AND BOTH LOTS CAME TO THE APPOINTED DAY BEFORE GOD FOR ALL NATIONS: That is to say, both lots fell on the same day appointed by God from eternity, namely the 13th and 14th of Adar, or February; but this day was happy and joyful for the Jews, whereas for the Gentile enemies of the Jews it was unlucky and fatal.

BECAUSE THE LORD REMEMBERED HIS INHERITANCE: That is, the people of Israel, whom God singularly loves as His inheritance and His own possession, and therefore protects, saves and defends. Symbol. St. Prosper, book 1 on the calling of the Gentiles.

For God chose the Jews as faithful ones unto grace and glory, from whom He excluded the unfaithful gentiles; yet in such a way that He did not deny them the common course of His providence and grace, as Prosper shows in the same place.

But for the Jews faithful to Him, He performed great signs and portents among the Gentiles: as when through Moses He afflicted Egypt with ten plagues, when through Joshua He subjugated the Canaanites, through David the Philistines, etc. It is to these portents that reference is made here.
# GEM CAPUT UNDECIMUM - SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER

Anno quarto, regnantibus Ptolemaeo et Cleopatra: in Egypt. For in that age, from Ptolemy I son of Lagus, king of Egypt, all the rest were called Ptolemies; and the queens Cleopatras. This Ptolemy was he who was surnamed Philometor, sixth from Alexander the Great, and greatly favored the Jews; indeed he committed the entire governance of his kingdom to them, and appointed as commanders of his army the Jews Onias and Dositheus, as Josephus testifies, book 2 Against Apion. It is uncertain whether this Dositheus is the same as Dositheus the priest, who brought to Jerusalem the epistle of Purim (plurima, that is, of the lots), written in Hebrew by Mordecai and translated into Greek by Lysimachus, as is said here. Furthermore this verse was not written by Mordecai, nor was it added by the Septuagint translators, for they lived before Philometor under Ptolemy Philadelphus, but was inserted by some later hagiographic scribe for historical purposes.

Anno secundo: Here begins the epistle of Mordecai, which was written in the second year of Artaxerxes, that is, of Darius Hystaspes ruling in Persia. For Artaxerxes was the common name of the kings of Persia, just as Ahasuerus was of the Medes, as I have said. For it was the same year in which Darius granted the Jews the opportunity to resume the building of the Temple which Cyrus had begun but whose son Cambyses had halted, as is clear from 1 Esdras, chapters 4 and 6.
Note on the prologue: This chapter ought to be placed at the beginning of the book in historical order, as the Greek and Roman editions place it.

Et hoc ejus somnium fuit: Apparuerunt voces, et tumultus, et tonitrua, et terrae motus, et conturbatio super terram: All these things signified the tumults and disturbances which Haman stirred up against the Jews through the edict commanding all to be killed. For there were many hundreds of thousands of Jews who were all to be slain along with their wives, daughters, and handmaids. Consider how great must have been the consternation, tumult, and lamentation among them.
Paratique contra se in praelium: In Greek: Both parties came forth to fight, namely Mordecai against Haman.
Ut pugnarent contra gentem justorum: that is, against the Jews, who were the faithful people dedicated to the worship of God.

Fons parvus crevit in fluvium maximum: The small fountain grew into a great river — this signifies Esther, who though she was but one lowly woman, by her intercession before the king became as a great river, bringing salvation to all the Jewish people.

Lux et sol ortus est; et humiles (Judaei) exaltati sunt, et devoraverunt inclytos: The light and sun arose; and the humble (Jews) were exalted and devoured the mighty — that is, the Jews who had been humbled and condemned to death were raised up by divine favor and destroyed their powerful enemies, above all Haman and his sons.
Aman with his followers, and the rest of those who opposed them.

The small fountain was Esther with her Jews, who at first grew as something lowly and slender into a river, indeed was converted into light and the sun, when having been made queen she raised up Mardocheus and the Jews, so that they might triumph over Aman and their enemies by slaying them.

He alludes to small springs, which in deep places, such as wells and caverns, flow forth modestly, and are therefore obscure and dark; but when they grow by the influx of neighboring waters, they become clear and bright, according to that saying: 'The spring was clear, bright with shining silvery waters.'

Indeed they become radiant like the sun, especially when they are so struck by the rays of the sun that the sun expresses in them either a rainbow or itself, as happens in the parhelion, when the sun shining through thin watery clouds displays another sun as it were to be seen in them.
For in like manner Esther after obtaining the kingdom shone with majesty and glory like the sun. See what was said on the preceding chapter, verse 7.
HE HAD THOUGHT UPON WHAT GOD WOULD DO, AND HAD FIXED IN HIS MIND (to investigate and search out the meaning of the dream), WISHING TO KNOW WHAT IT MIGHT SIGNIFY.
Hence it is clear that Mardocheus, when he first saw the dream, did not understand its meaning, but came to know it gradually from the outcome of events and by the instinct of God.
Yet he perceived in a general way that something great was being signified to him by God in favor of the Jews.
Whence Josippus son of Gorion, book 2, says: When Aman was so hostile to Mardocheus, he said to his Esther: The dream which I told you in the days of your youth is being fulfilled.
CHAPTER TWELVE. SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER. MARDOCHEUS REVEALS TO ASSUERUS THROUGH ESTHER THE CONSPIRACY OF THE EUNUCHS AGAINST HIM.

What follows was placed in that location where it is written in the book: "And they plundered their goods or possessions." This we find only in the Vulgate edition. The example of the letters was as follows.
Now Mordecai at that time was in the court of the king, with Bagatha and Thara, eunuchs of the king, who were gatekeepers of the palace.
And when he had learned of their thoughts and had observed their plots more diligently, he discovered that they were attempting to lay hands upon King Artaxerxes, and he reported this to the king.
The king, having examined both men, ordered them when they had confessed to be led to death.
But the king wrote what had been done in the records; and Mordecai also committed the memory of the matter to writing.

And the king commanded him to remain in the court of the palace, gifts having been given to him for the information. # GEM — Note on "gifts given for the information": these were small and of little importance, for afterward greater ones were given, as is clear from chapter 6, verse 3. Or rather this is a prolepsis, that is, an anticipation: for these gifts appear to have been given to Mordecai not immediately after the denunciation, but long afterward, namely when the king, reading the Annals, understood that no reward had been given to Mordecai for the denunciation, as is narrated in chapter 6, verse 3.
But Haman, the son of Hammedatha the Agagite, was most glorious before the king, and wished to harm Mordecai and his people, on account of the two eunuchs of the king who had been slain.

Why is Haman called "Bugaeus"? Some answer: he is called Bugaeus, that is, son of Bogis. For Xerxes held Bogis in high honor, as Herodotus attests in books 7 and 8; and that Boges was also a Macedonian, as Haman was, is clear from the fact that Herodotus adds that he was born in the city of Sinn, that is, Amphipolis, which is a city of Macedonia, past which flows the river Armon. But these events occurred before Xerxes, under Darius the father of Xerxes, as I showed in the Prooemium. I say therefore: Bugaeus, or as some read, Ugaeus, is the same as Agagaeus, that is, descended from the stock of Agag, king of Amalek. That this is so is clear from the fact that the word "Bugaeus" is not in the Hebrew but is taken from the Septuagint.

The edict of Artaxerxes decreed that throughout the whole world there was a people dispersed, which used new laws, and acting contrary to the customs of all nations, despised the commands of kings, and by its dissension violated the harmony of all peoples.

Lapide notes that Haman, set over all the provinces and second to the king, whom the king honored as a father, was ordered to point out whomever should be destroyed with their wives and children, with none showing mercy.
The fourteenth day of the twelfth month Adar was set as the day of destruction, so that these wicked men descending to hell in one day would restore peace to the empire which they had disturbed.

Lapide notes that what follows after the copy of the letter was found written in the place where one reads: "And Mordecai departing did all that Esther had commanded him," yet these things are not found in the Hebrew, nor are they cited by any of the interpreters.
Mordecai prayed to the Lord, mindful of all His works.
He said: Lord, Lord, omnipotent King, for all things are placed in Thy power, and there is none who can resist Thy will, if Thou dost decree to save Israel.
Thou hast made heaven and earth, and whatsoever is contained within the circuit of heaven.
Thou art Lord of all, and there is none who can resist Thy majesty.
Thou knowest all things, and Thou knowest that not out of pride or contumely, nor out of any desire for glory, did I do this, namely that I would not adore Haman the most proud.

For I would willingly, for the salvation of Israel, have been ready even to kiss the soles of his feet. # GEM Lapide notes this beautiful protestation of Mordecai, showing his refusal to adore Haman was not from stubbornness but from piety toward God alone.
But I feared lest I should transfer the honor of my God to a man, and lest I should adore anyone except my God.
And now, O Lord, King, God of Abraham, have mercy on Thy people, because our enemies desire to destroy us and to blot out Thy inheritance.
Despise not Thy portion, which Thou hast redeemed to Thyself out of Egypt.
Hear my supplication, and be propitious to Thy lot and portion, and turn our mourning into joy, that living we may praise Thy name, O Lord, and close not the mouths of those who sing to Thee. # GEM
All Israel likewise with one mind and supplication cried out to the Lord, because certain death hung over them.

Lapide notes on "let them be destroyed on the fourteenth day of the twelfth month Adar": for on the thirteenth day they were commanded to begin the slaughter, but on the fourteenth to complete it, as determined by lot.

Lapide notes on "living in silence," that is, in quiet without tumults and disturbances, passing life in peace and tranquility, as the Greek text has it; and this pertains to the canonical Scripture according to the decree of the Council of Trent, session 4.

Lapide notes on "for I would willingly for the salvation of Israel even be ready to kiss the soles of his feet": this shows that Mordecai's refusal was not from arrogance but purely from fear of transferring divine honor to a mere man.
This chapter recounts the events of the thirteenth and fourteenth days of Adar, when the Jews struck down their enemies throughout the Persian empire. # GEM

S. Jerome prefaces the prayer of Mordecai with the note: "These things are not found in the Hebrew, that is, in the shorter version used by S. Jerome, nor are they found among any of the interpreters, namely Aquila, Symmachus, Theodotion, the fifth and sixth editions, and similar ones."

The Septuagint must be excepted, since S. Jerome translated these passages from it; for before his time the Septuagint version was the Vulgate used by the Church, and S. Jerome himself acknowledges in the prologue that he translated these things from the Vulgate.
Moreover, the prayer of Mordecai is pious, vigorous, and fervent, and accordingly the Church employs it in the Mass on the Wednesday after the second Sunday.
This prayer of Mordecai is recited in this verse; before it S. Jerome notes: "These things are not found in the Hebrew."
The term translated as "footsteps" or "traces" (vestigium) is taken metonymically for the soles of the feet and the soles (plantae pedum) by which these traces are impressed upon the earth.

Hence Josippus Gorionides writes: "Would that I had been worthy to lick the sandal which is on his feet, and to wash what he himself treads upon with his feet"; and similarly: "They shall adore thee with face cast down to earth, and shall lick the dust of thy feet," as in Psalm 71:9: "The Ethiopians shall fall down before him, and his enemies shall lick the dust."

Serarius and Sanchez, with many examples, show that vestigium (footstep) is frequently taken for the foot pressing upon the ground, that is, for the sole of the foot (planta pedis), from which it gathers dirt and filth; to kiss this is an act of the utmost abasement and veneration.
But I feared lest I should transfer to him the honor of my God — that is, the worship of latria due to God alone — "and transfer it to a man."
Esther too, the queen, fled to the Lord in fear of the danger that threatened her. #GEM

When she had put off her royal garments, she took on garments fitted for weeping and mourning, and instead of various ointments she filled her head with ashes and dung, and humiliated her body with fastings: and all the places wherein before she was wont to rejoice, she filled with the tearing of her hair.

Humiliated with fastings: the word "fastings" is not in the Greek, but is understood; for the Hebrews call the humiliation of the body a fast, for nothing so brings down the flesh as fasting; whence the Hebrews call the fast proclaimed by Esther on the 13th of Adar "taanith Esther," that is, humiliation, that is, the fast of Esther. Wisely says St. Ambrose, Epistle 88: Esther, he says, inclined the proud king by her fasts.

With the tearing of her hair: the ancients were accustomed in great calamity and lamentation to clap the hands, beat the breast, pluck the cheeks, and tear the hair from grief of heart, so as to move all to compassion and weeping, as Ezra narrates of himself, 1 Ezra 9, and Josephus relates of Alexandra the daughter of Hyrcanus and mother-in-law of Herod, and Salome the sister of Herod, lib. 15 c. 11 and lib. 16 c. 11.
And she prayed to the Lord God of Israel, saying: O my Lord, who art our King alone, help me a desolate woman, who have no other helper but thee.

Help me desolate: both because I am alone a Jewess in the court of a gentile king; and because I am destitute of all human aid, as follows; and because I desire the address and consolation of no man, but of God alone. Josippus says Esther prayed thus: I dwell here alone, I am alone in the king's house without father and without mother. Like a poor little orphan who begs from door to door, so I beg thy mercies from window to window, going from one to another to look toward heaven, and thence to await help in so great affliction from God; for this reason the Jews were accustomed when praying to ascend to the rooftops and look toward heaven through the windows, as Judith did, c. 8.5, and Daniel, c. 6.10; indeed Christ for this reason ascended mountains, and there spent the night in prayer to God, Luke 6.12.
My danger is in my hands.

My danger is in my hands (Josippus: my soul is in my hands), that is, it is most present and extreme; for that which is carried in the hands can easily be spilled, or seized and taken away by a thief or enemy. So David, fleeing from Saul and from enemies seeking to kill him, says, Psalm 118 v. 109: My soul is in my hands, that is, my life is in the greatest danger, so that I seem to carry it in my hand and offer it to anyone wishing to take it.

I have heard, O Lord, from my father (Deut. 4.2.3) that thou, O Lord, didst take Israel from all the nations, and our fathers from all their forebears, to possess an everlasting inheritance, and didst do to them as thou hadst promised.
We have sinned in thy sight, and therefore thou hast delivered us into the hands of our enemies:
For we have worshipped their gods. Thou art just, O Lord.
For we have worshipped their gods: not I, but our ancestors in Jerusalem under Manasseh and other idolatrous kings. So Josippus Gorionides.
And now it does not suffice them that we are oppressed with most hard bondage, but attributing the strength of their hands to the power of their idols,

Attributing the strength of their hands to the power of their idols: that is, as Josippus translates: Behold, they themselves (Haman and his followers) do not say that thou, O Lord, hast delivered us into their hands, but ascribe it to their idols (Mithras, that is, the sun).
They wish to change thy promises, and destroy thy inheritance, and stop the mouths of them that praise thee, and extinguish the glory of thy temple and altar,

They wish to change thy promises (by which thou didst promise to our fathers Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, etc. that thou wouldst be our God and protector) "and to destroy thy inheritance" (that is, the people of Israel, who as thy peculiar people acknowledge and worship thee alone as their God and Lord) "and to extinguish the glory of thy temple and altar": recently rebuilt in Jerusalem by this same Ahasuerus, that is Darius son of Hystaspes, 1 Ezra 6. For if Haman had persuaded Darius to destroy all the Jews in Persia, his hatred would not have stopped there, but he would have caused Jerusalem and the temple to be overthrown as well.
That they may open the mouths of the nations, and praise the strength of idols, and proclaim a carnal king for ever.
And proclaim a carnal king: Darius or Haman, who dominates us like a king and tyrant, so that they proclaim him to have slain and destroyed us all, as more powerful than us and our God.
O Lord, give not thy sceptre to them that are not, lest they laugh at our ruin: but turn their counsel upon themselves, and destroy him that has begun to rage against us.

Give not thy sceptre to those who are not: that is, to false gods and idols who are not gods, so that thy divinity and omnipotence should be ascribed to them; as if to say: Suffer not divine power to be attributed to demons as though they were gods, as though Haman who worships them had through that power destroyed us, so that demons might boast over thee, O Lord. #GEM
