Remember, O Lord, and shew thyself to us in the time of our tribulation, and give me boldness, O Lord, King of gods and of all power.
Give me a well-ordered speech in my mouth in the presence of the lion, and turn his heart to the hatred of our enemy, that both he himself may perish, and the rest that consent to him.
But deliver us by thy hand, and help me, who have no other helper but thee, O Lord, who hast the knowledge of all things.
And thou knowest that I hate the glory of the wicked, and abhor the bed of the uncircumcised, and of every stranger.
Esther here protests before God that she does not take pleasure in the royal dignity or in the king's bed, so that she may be excused from any suspicion of sin. #GEM

Thou knowest my necessity, that I abominate the sign of my pride and glory, which is upon my head in the days of my ostentation, and detest it as a menstruous rag, and wear it not in the days of my silence.

The sign of my pride and glory which is upon my head: that is, the royal crown, which Esther wore when she appeared in public before the king and his court, but which she detested as a menstruous rag and did not wear in private, in the days of her silence and retirement, offering this to God as a proof of her contempt for worldly glory.
And that I have not eaten at Haman's table, nor hath the king's banquet pleased me, and that I have not drunk the wine of the drink offerings:
And that thy handmaid hath never rejoiced, since I was brought hither unto this present day, but in thee, O Lord God of Abraham.
O God, mighty above all, hear the voice of them that have no other hope, and deliver us from the hand of the wicked, and deliver me from my fear.
Mordecai commanded Esther to go in to the king and entreat him for her people and her country. # GEM
Remember, he said, "the days of your lowliness, how you were nurtured in my hand, because Haman, second to the king, has spoken against us unto death."
Invoke the Lord, and speak to the king for us, and deliver us from death.
On the third day she put off her garments of adornment and was clothed in her glory.
And when she shone in her royal attire and had invoked God, the ruler and savior of all, she took two handmaids.
Upon one she leaned, as if unable to bear her own body by reason of her delicacy and excessive tenderness.
The other handmaid followed her mistress, bearing up the garments that flowed down to the ground.
She herself, her face suffused with rosy color and her eyes gracious and bright, concealed a sorrowful spirit contracted with excessive fear.

Having passed in order through all the doors, she stood before the king where he sat upon his royal throne, clothed in royal robes and gleaming with gold and precious stones, and he was terrible to behold.
When he had lifted up his face and with burning eyes had shown the fury of his breast, the queen fell, and changing color to paleness, she rested her weary head upon her handmaid.
But God turned the spirit of the king to gentleness, and in haste and fear he leapt from his throne and sustained her in his arms until she returned to herself, soothing her with these words.
What is the matter, Esther? I am your brother; do not be afraid.
You shall not die; for this law is not established for you, but for all others. Come near therefore.
Approach and touch the scepter.
When she was silent, he took the golden rod and placed it upon her neck and kissed her, saying: "Why do you not speak to me?"
She answered: "I saw you, my lord, as an angel of God, and my heart was troubled for fear of your glory." # GEM
For you are wonderful, my lord, and your face is full of grace.
And while she spoke, she fell again and was nearly lifeless.
But the king was troubled, and all his ministers comforted her.
This chapter is inserted between chapters 5:2; for what was said more briefly there, is here recounted more fully.

THAT I MAY BE IN THY HAND, that is, through me, and my help and work: although properly infants are carried in the hand and bosom of nurses (such as Mordecai had been to Esther), and are nourished there.
HE HATH SPOKEN AGAINST US UNTO DEATH, that is, unto death, so that he condemned us to death through the king's edict.

SHE PUT OFF HER GARMENTS OF GLORY, that is, the modest and mournful garments with which she had been clothed and adorned for mourning and penitence. Whence in Greek it reads: She put off the garments of mourning.

SHE WAS COMPASSED ABOUT WITH HER GLORY, that is, she clothed herself with the glorious royal crown and other golden and jewelled garments, as befitted royalty. Thus Job 19:9 says: He hath stripped me of my glory, which he explains by adding: he hath taken the crown from my head; for Job was a king and was crowned with a royal crown.

AND UPON ONE SHE LEANED, AS THOUGH FROM LUXURY (in Greek ὡς τρυφερευομένη, that is, as one acting delicately, conducting herself in a delicate manner), "AND UNABLE TO BEAR HER OWN BODY FROM EXCESSIVE TENDERNESS." Esther assumed this appearance of weakness in order to move Ahasuerus to pity. And truly she was tender and feeble, both from her sex, and from fasting, and from fear; whence also she fell into a swoon.

SHE HID HER TROUBLED SOUL AND ITS EXCESSIVE FEAR; # GEM for she knew that Darius was terrible, and that his edict concerning the slaughter of the Jews, already issued and proclaimed, could not by the law of the Persians be revoked; moreover that it was a capital offence to approach the king unless one had been summoned by him. Wherefore, knowing that she faced certain mortal danger, she was full of fear and trembling lest the matter should end badly, and the king, flaring up in anger, should immediately order his edict concerning the killing of the Jews to be carried out. Whence there follows:

HAVING THEREFORE PASSED THROUGH ALL THE DOORS IN ORDER, SHE STOOD BEFORE THE KING. It is probable that the trembling Esther did not approach near to the king, but being terrified by his dreadful aspect, stood at a distance at the door which was opposite the king.
[On the outer court of the royal house, which was outside facing the basilica of the king.]

When he lifted up his face and with burning eyes showed the fury of his breast: Josippus as well as Josephus says this fury arose from the fact that Esther had entered to him unbidden and contrary to law, since he supposed himself to be held in contempt by Esther.
For the kings of the Persians bore before them a great, indeed divine majesty, and demanded great reverence — indeed, latria — from their subjects, wishing to be adored as gods. # GEM
Added to this was the fact that Darius was by nature choleric and fierce, as is clear from what follows.

[Lapide notes that when Darius waged war against the Scythians surrounding the East, he was asked by the noble Obazo to leave one of three sons as comfort for his father while using the other two; Darius promised more than was asked, saying he would return all three—then slew them before the father's eyes, saying he would have been cruel had he taken all away. A similar story is told of Xerxes son of Darius: when Pythius begged that one of his five sons be spared from the call-up, and was allowed to choose which one he wished, Xerxes had the chosen son cut in two and placed one half on each side of the road, and thus consecrated his army with this victim.]

[On "the queen collapsed": Some suspect she feigned a fainting spell to soften the king's anger. But what preceded and what followed indicates the faint was genuine. For she had approached already seized with excessive fear; then, seeing the king in such majesty gazing at her with flashing and furious eyes, she was struck with trembling and fell as though lifeless. For royal majesty wonderfully strikes those who approach it. Grave men have related—men who feared no one else and were accustomed to drink and deal familiarly with princes—that when they approached Philip II king of Spain to petition something, and the king fixed his eyes upon them with royal gravity, they were struck dumb with consternation, and though the king courteously invited them to state their business, they could not utter a single word.] # GEM

[On v.11: "And God turned the spirit of the king to mildness": He turned it both through the pitiful sight of the queen collapsing, and through an interior instinct and impulse by which He inclined him toward compassion, to change his anger into mildness and tenderness. For the heart of the king is in the hand of the Lord; whithersoever he will, he shall turn it (Prov. 21:2); especially because Ahasuerus saw that Esther had not approached out of contempt as he had supposed, but held him in supreme fear and reverence—for this is what kings seek—and that she had fallen and fainted from very reverence and trembling.]

[On v.12: "What is the matter, Esther? I am your brother": Brother, that is, husband, who loves you not as a master and lord but as a brother, companion and equal. Note that spouses sometimes address one another as brother and sister, partly for the sake of modesty and chastity, partly for the sake of good will and close conjugal love, especially because the first marriages in the world after Adam and Eve were between brothers and sisters. So the bride in the Canticle is called sister by the bridegroom; so wisdom is called the bride of the wise man (Wisd. 8:2), and is named both friend and sister (Prov. 7:4). Finally—and this is peculiar to this passage—the Persians were accustomed to take their sisters as wives, as St. Jerome attests (Adversus Jovinianum, bk. 2); the same is taught from Herodotus, Agathias, Clement of Alexandria, and Diogenes Laertius by Brissonius (De regno Persarum, bk. 2, p. 214).]

[On v.15: "And he kissed her": as a beloved bride, to restore her by these endearments as though from death. Esther here represents the Blessed Virgin, who, approaching Ahasuerus—that is, God angered at the whole human race on account of the sins of Adam and his posterity—with two handmaids, that is, with the twofold creature, namely angelic and human, as St. Bonaventure says in the Speculum (ch. 3); or mystically, with the twofold virtue and life, namely active and contemplative; and beseeching him, she falls as into a faint from sacred awe and reverence of the divine majesty; but God, caressing and kissing her, granted what she sought, namely the salvation of the human race, and therefore gave her His Son, who, taking flesh in her, became the propitiatory victim and Redeemer of the world. This is what she herself was asking in Canticles 1:1: Let him kiss me with the kiss of his mouth.] # GEM

[On v.16: "I saw you, O Lord, as an Angel of God": shining with angelic majesty and splendor, and therefore inspiring fear and terror in me and in the others. Hence Chosroes king of the Persians, a pagan whom the Emperor Heraclius conquered, ordered a wondrous sphere to be constructed beside him in which angels stood around the king. Hear Theophanes on this...]
Rex magnus Artaxerxes: This is Ahasuerus, called Artaxerxes in the Greek version of Esther. He writes now to the 127 provinces, reversing his former edict against the Jews. See above, ch. 11, v. 2.

Multi bonitate principum abusi sunt in superbiam: The king begins with a general maxim drawn from experience: that many men, elevated by the goodness of princes, abuse that honour and swell into pride. This is a commonplace of political wisdom, confirmed by daily observation.

Et non solum subjectos regibus nituntur opprimere: Not content with oppressing those beneath them, such ungrateful men turn their ambition even against the kings who raised them. So Aman plotted against Ahasuerus himself after being made second in the realm.

Nec contenti sunt gratias non agere beneficiis: They do not merely fail in gratitude; they go further and think themselves able to escape even the judgment of God who sees all things. This is the height of impiety — ingratitude compounded with contempt of divine providence. # GEM

Et in tantum vesaniae proruperunt: Such men reach such madness that they strive by underground tunnels of lies — cuniculis mendaciorum, a vivid metaphor — to undermine and destroy those who faithfully discharge their duties and deserve universal praise.

Dum aures principum simplices: Princes, whose own nature is simple and candid, judging others by themselves, are easily deceived by the crafty fraud of wicked flatterers. Hence kings must be vigilant against evil counsellors. # GEM

Quae res et ex veteribus probatur historiis: This truth is confirmed both by ancient histories and by daily events, showing how the good intentions of kings are perverted by the evil suggestions of certain men around them.
Unde providendum est paci omnium provinciarum: Therefore the king has a duty to provide for the peace of all provinces, and to correct errors when they become known, however they arose.

Nec putare debetis, si diversa jubeamus, ex animi nostri venire levitate: The king excuses his apparent inconsistency — issuing now a contrary decree — by appeal not to fickleness of mind but to the quality and necessity of the times and the utility of the commonwealth. A wise and honest apology.

Aman filius Amadathi, et animo et gente Macedo: Aman is here called a Macedonian both in spirit and in race, that is, alien to the Persians, treacherous, and ambitious. See above, ch. 3, v. 2. Jerome and others note that the Macedonians were proverbially arrogant and cruel.

Et tantam in se expertus humanitatem ut pater noster vocaretur: Aman had received from Ahasuerus the extraordinary honour of being called his father and of being adored by all as second after the king. Such was the king's munificence, and such was Aman's ingratitude.

Qui in tantum arrogantiae tumorem sublatus est: Lifted to such a swelling of arrogance that he endeavoured to deprive the king both of his kingdom and of his very life. Pride is the root of all treachery against benefactors. # GEM

Nam Mardochaeum, cujus fide et beneficiis vivimus: The king here acknowledges that he lives by the fidelity and benefits of Mordecai, who had discovered the conspiracy against his life. He names Esther as consort of the kingdom, and shows that Aman sought the death of both together with all their people.

Hoc cogitans ut, illis interfectis, insidiaretur nostrae solitudini: Aman's deeper plot was that after destroying Mordecai and Esther he might attack the king in his isolation and transfer the Persian kingdom to the Macedonians. Thus private malice served a vast political ambition.

Nos autem, a pessimo mortalium Judaeos neci destinatos: The king confesses that the Jews were condemned to death by the worst of mortals, Aman, and that upon examination they were found entirely without fault. A remarkable public vindication of an innocent people.

Filios altissimi et maximi, semperque viventis Dei: The king calls the Jews sons of the most high, greatest, and ever-living God, by whose benefit the kingdom was given to the Persian forefathers and preserved to this day. This is a noble confession of the true God by a pagan king, perhaps prompted by Esther and Mordecai. # GEM

Unde eas litteras, quas non nomine nostro ille direxerat, sciatis esse irritas: The former letters sent under the royal name by Aman's fraud are declared null and void. The king thus repudiates the earlier edict of destruction as issued without true royal authority.

Pro quo scelere ante portas hujus urbis, id est, Susan, et ipse qui machinatus est pendet in patibulo: Aman and all his kindred hang on gibbets before the gates of Susa. The king adds significantly: non nobis, sed Deo reddente ei quod meruit — not we but God rendered to him what he deserved. A pious acknowledgment of divine retributive justice. # GEM

Hoc autem edictum in cunctis urbibus proponatur, ut liceat Judaeis uti legibus suis: The new edict grants the Jews full liberty to live according to their own laws throughout all the provinces of the empire. This is a reversal not only of the sentence of death but of all religious and civil oppression.

Quibus debetis esse adminiculо, ut eos qui se ad necem eorum paraverant possint interficere: The provincial officials are commanded to assist the Jews so that they may be able to kill those who had prepared themselves to slay them, on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, Adar. Thus the day of intended massacre becomes the day of Jewish triumph.

Hanc enim diem Deus omnipotens moeroris et luctus eis vertit in gaudium: God Almighty turned this day from mourning and grief into joy. The king attributes this reversal explicitly to divine omnipotence. This is the theological heart of the chapter: God's providential governance of history. # GEM

Unde et vos, inter caeteros festos dies, hanc habetote diem et celebrate eam cum omni laetitia: Hence this day is to be kept as a feast among all other festivals, celebrated with all gladness, so that in times to come it may be known — this is the feast of Purim, instituted here by royal decree and confirmed by Mordecai and Esther in the Hebrew text.

Omnes qui fideliter Persis obediunt: All who faithfully obey the Persians are to observe this feast, that posterity may know the reward of fidelity and the punishment of treachery. The edict thus serves both a religious and a political purpose, binding all subjects of the empire to honour the day of Jewish deliverance.

# GEM On the words "WHILE THEY DECEIVE THE SIMPLE EARS OF PRINCES, WHO BY THEIR NATURE JUDGE OTHERS BY THEMSELVES, WITH CUNNING FRAUD": Hence let Princes and any others learn not to give easy credence to accusations and accusers, nor to confide too much in one counsellor; but let them hear many, if they wish to know the truth; for "he that is hasty to believe is light of heart, and shall be lessened," Ecclus. 19:4.

Thus Joshua and the Hebrews were deceived by the Gibeonites, because they too easily believed them when they lied. Thus Holofernes was struck down by Judith, because he rashly believed her. Thus Samson was betrayed by his Delilah, because he believed her without caution with the secret of his strength in his hair. Potiphar rashly believed his wife accusing Joseph of fornication; and Absalom believed Hushai; and Rehoboam believed the young men; and therefore they were deceived and brought to ruin. Finally Haman coveted the kingdom of Ahasuerus, for the reason that Ahasuerus had entrusted the whole of it to him. Others have done the same in former times, and also in our own age, who, while they exercised dominion over kings and princes, invaded their power and kingdoms.

On the words "NOR OUGHT YOU TO THINK, IF WE COMMAND DIFFERENT THINGS (by revoking Haman's decree for the slaying of the Jews), THAT THIS COMES FROM THE LEVITY OF OUR MIND": lest we lightly violate or abrogate the law of the Persians, which commands the decrees of kings to be irrevocable; for the earlier decree was not the king's but Haman's, who extorted it from the king by fraud and falsehood, and therefore it was subreptitious, unjust and impious, and accordingly, the truth now being known, it is to be revoked and corrected by the king by this new edict.

On "HAMAN, A MACEDONIAN BY RACE": For although he was born in Persia, yet he drew his stock and origin from the Macedonians, and accordingly was disposed toward the Macedonians as toward his own countrymen, and therefore was alien and hostile to the Persians, and was craftily endeavouring to transfer the kingdom from them to his own Macedonians, as follows.
On "AND WITH HIS LIFE," that is, with his very life.

On "THAT HAVING SLAIN (the Jews, and especially Esther and Mordecai, my most faithful friends and guardians of my life) HE MIGHT PLOT AGAINST OUR SOLITUDE": so that, having bereft me of such trusty guardians, as if left alone and lacking faithful protection, he might by guile and fraud slay me and seize my kingdom. That this is true is clear from the fact that this letter was dictated not so much by the king as by Esther and Mordecai, who were upright, truthful and God-fearing, and who, through Haman's secret letters and by questioning and diligent examination of his accomplices, detected and demonstrated the frauds and conspiracy of Haman against the king.

Every province and city that shall refuse to be partaker of this solemnity, let it perish by the sword and by fire; and be so utterly destroyed that it become impassable not only to men but even to beasts forever, as an example of contempt and disobedience.

[On the transfer of the Persian kingdom to the Macedonians] Scaliger and Serarius think that Ahasuerus, husband of Esther, was not Artaxerxes Longimanus but Artaxerxes Mnemon, under whom lived Philip, father of Alexander the Great, who began to amplify and glorify the kingdom of the Macedonians, which until then had been small and obscure.

Yet I say that by "Macedonians" here we should understand the Greeks, because the Macedonians were preeminent among them; they are named here before all other Greeks because Haman was a Macedonian, who therefore would have preferred to see himself or some other Macedonian reign over the Persians rather than an Athenian, Spartan, or other Greek. Herodotus (bk. 7, p. 183) teaches that the Greeks under Darius Hystaspis were to be feared and had done harm to the Persians.

Diodorus Siculus (bk. 11) writes that Xerxes in the 75th Olympiad took up arms against the Greeks, who were already at that time the constant and most fierce enemies of the Persian name; wherefore Xerxes led against them 1,100,000 Persians, all of whom were slain, routed, and put to flight by a handful of Greeks. He adds that Xerxes' father Darius had prepared great materials for the building of the fleet of Xerxes to be sent against the Greeks.

Justin (bk. 2, pp. 34 and 39) likewise teaches that Darius Hystaspis waged wars against the Athenians, in which war he asserts that the virtue and glory of the young Themistocles already shone forth. Finally, four years before his death, this Darius fought against the Greeks at Marathon, in a battle most celebrated in Greek and Roman histories, as Sulpicius Severus also noted (bk. 2, p. 68).

Josephus Gorionides agrees, and adds that those two eunuchs who conspired against Ahasuerus, and were therefore denounced by Mordecai and hanged, had intended to bring the head of Ahasuerus or Artaxerxes to the king of the Greeks, to ingratiate themselves with him; and that these eunuchs were kinsmen of Haman. Because at that time the empire of the Macedonians was still most obscure (being long before Philip and Alexander), Haman the Macedonian wished to glorify it by transferring to himself and his Macedonians the most noble empire — indeed the monarchy — of Darius and the Persians, just as Alexander afterwards did transfer it.

[The kingdom has been given by God to our fathers and to us] By "fathers" understand the preceding kings, especially Cyrus, to whom God foretold and promised the kingdom through Isaiah ch. 44-45, and Cyrus acknowledged this, and therefore sent the Jews free from Babylon (1 Ezra 1:1). This same Darius likewise acknowledged it, as is plain from this passage and from 3 Ezra 4, v. 42 and following. # GEM

[And his kindred hang on gibbets] The word means kindred, that is, kinsmen; for the sons of Haman were slain and hanged in the ninth month after this edict, namely in the last month Adar, as I said at ch. 9:13; because, as Ammianus (bk. 25) says, the laws among the Persians were exceedingly dreaded, inasmuch as by them on account of the guilt of one person all his kindred perished.

[Among other feast days] For the Persians had their own feasts, especially those dedicated to Mithra, that is, the Sun, whom they worshipped as a god. Such also was the feast on account of the slaughter of the Magi, which this Darius, succeeding the Magi in the kingdom, had ordained to be celebrated perpetually, as Herodotus (bk. 3) witnesses. Moreover, it is not surprising that Darius ordered this feast of the Jews' victory to be celebrated even by the Eastern Persians, both because he himself was wholly dependent on his Esther and Mordecai, and because he had already acknowledged and worshipped the true God of the Hebrews, as is clear from ch. 10 above.
